AAOIFI - World Bank 13th Annual Conference
“THE ROLE OF STANDARDIZATION FOR
THE FUTURE OF ISLAMIC FINANCE”
Tuesday & Wednesday 14-15 Safar 1440H, 23-24 October 2018

Diplomat Hotel, Kingdom of Bahrain

Agenda of 
AAOIFI – World Bank 13th Annual Conference
“The Role of Standardization 
for the Future of Islamic Finance”
23-24 October 2018, Kingdom of Bahrain

The Islamic finance industry has witnessed remarkable growth, reach, resilience, and innovation over the past 
few decades. Today, it stands at around USD 3 trillion mark. There is consensus amongst experts that the industry 
size will continue to balloon further and show upward trend in the future primarily due to the fact that 70% of 
the unbanked 1.5 billion Muslim populace is fast becoming receptive to and accepting of the Shari’ah compliance 
proposition which has prompted different jurisdictions— including the non-Muslim ones—to not only participate 
in but also vie for becoming global and regional Islamic finance hubs. However, change is the norm of the day, 
and the world is fast changing. It is widely realized that the same of more cannot continue indefinitely especially 
in the wake of ongoing creative disruption of the banking business model and regulation through revolutionary 
technologies such as Blockchain, cryptocurrencies and smart contracts, as well as the implementation of VAT in 
the GCC states. But the battle for the future of Islamic finance is being fought at yet another front which is of far 
more significance than any other: against the threat of reputational risk due to actual or perceived non-compliance 
with  Shari’ah  which  has  the  potential  to  stagnate,  if  not  harm,  the  industry.  The  only  safeguard  proposed  for 
reputational risk is the push for global standardization of Shari’ah and accounting practices. But how would we 
operationalize the standardization agenda? Surely, that would require a collaborative effort from all stakeholders.

Welcome  to  the  13th  edition  of  the AAOIFI  –  World  Bank  Conference  where  the  future  of  the  Islamic  finance 
industry and critically, the role of standardization in shaping it, will be discussed, debated, and deconstructed 
through the perspectives of leading experts and scholars, representatives of central banks, regulatory, supervisory 
authorities and practitioners.

  Session 1: “Learning from the Wisdom” 
HowdoyouseetheroleofstandardizationinshapingthefutureoftheIslamicfinance
industry?

   H.E. Dr. Abdussalam Al-Abbadi, Secretary General, the Islamic Fiqh Academy of 
the Organization of the Islamic Conference

   H.E. Dr. Mohammad Ali Elgari, Chairman and Member of various Shari’ah Boards 
of IFIs, Member, Board of Trustees, AAOIFI

We  are  poised  at  this  initiatory  session  to  learn  from  the  wisdom  of  two  very  distinguished 
figures from the Islamic finance industry who will focus and enlighten the audience on how 
standardization can be taken yet further, with the collective efforts of major stakeholders, each 
in their own domain. This flagship face-to-face dialogue session will also shed light on the past, 
present, and future of the Islamic finance industry. Nothing will be off-the-table as the expert 
moderator will seek answers to the important questions, concerns, problems, and even criticism, 
which are on the industry’s mind. The audience will also have an opportunity to interact with 
these two distinguished figures in a Q&A session and benefit from their knowledge, experience, 
insight, and wisdom. 

  Moderator:

   Mr.  Sohaib  Umar,  Advisor  -  Islamic  Financial  Services  Development,  CBB  -  Member, 
Governance  and  Ethics  Board,  AAOIFI

  Main Topics:

   How significant is Islamic finance? For what purposes? For whom? 
   What has changed in Islamic finance in the last 35 years?
   Is Islamic finance really a success story? What is the benchmark to gauge and determine 
success? 
   What were the original aspirations of the Islamic finance industry? Has it remained true 
to itself?
   In your view is there any area(s) where Islamic finance has not been up to aspirations or 
expectations? How can this be changed to the better?
   What  does  standardization  of  Shari’ah  and  accounting  practices  mean  to  you?  How 
important  do  you  feel  is  it  for  the  future  of  Islamic  finance?
   How can the standardization agenda be promoted for maximum impact?
   What  is  the  actual  /  potential  role  of  OIC  Islamic  Fiqh Academy  in  promoting  Islamic 
Finance  in  local  economies  and  worldwide?
   How can AAOIFI and OIC Islamic Fiqh Academy set on cooperation in areas of mutual 
interest  (in  Fiqh  and  Shari’ah  domains)  to  the  best  interest  of  the  broader  Muslim 
community  and  the  industry?
   Where do you see Islamic finance 30 years from now?
   What is your message to the existing or future generation of Islamic finance regulators, 
Shari’ah scholars, and practitioners? Suggest one advise for each stakeholder.

11:45 – 1:30

   Session 2: Collaboration between Islamic Finance Infrastructure 
Institutions: Current Status and the Way Forward

Islamic  finance  infrastructure  institutions  have  separate  mandates  and  rarely  overstep  their 
boundaries. However, their vision is the same: to support the industry stakeholders and secure 
the future of Islamic finance. Can this shared vision become reason enough to witness higher, 
deeper,  wider,  and  stronger  levels  of  collaboration,  cooperation,  and  coordination  between 
infrastructure  institutions?  The  question  will  have  tremendous  implications  in  shaping  the 
future of Islamic finance.

  Main Topics:

   How can the Islamic finance industry benefit through collaboration between infrastructure 
bodies? Who stands to benefit the most?
   What is the current level of collaboration between infrastructure bodies? Is the future of 
industry is at stake due to the existing level of collaboration?
   Is there a need for transformational change in the current level of collaboration? How can 
such a change be initiated? Who will take the lead?
   What challenges and obstacles exist in the way of collaboration?
   What are the major potential avenues of collaboration – standards development, human 
resource development, research & development, etc.?
   How can the standardization agenda be promoted through collaboration efforts?

   Session 3: Islamic Finance  and  Cryptocurrencies - Governing 
and Regulating Cryptocurrencies 

One of the most important topics as well as the least understood-cryptocurrencies-is a hotly 
debated topic and will remain so until AAOIFI Shari’ah Board issues a clear Shari’ah ruling on 
it. In this session, however, the goal would be to focus on the governance and regulation related 
aspects of cryptocurrency, both as a standalone topic as well as within the context of Blockchain 
and other technologies, and to determine its role in the present and future of Islamic finance 
industry.

  Main Topics:

    What is the current dominant view on cryptocurrencies by leading Shari’ah scholars?
    What is the potential role of cryptocurrencies in Islamic finance? Will it have an impact on 
or influence its future for the better? Is it really relevant?
    What is the regulatory perspective on cryptocurrency? What type of opportunities and 
threats has it raised for regulators? 
    What are the key governance-related issues with cryptocurrencies for both regulators and 
Islamic financial institutions?
   What is the role of standardization in the context of cryptocurrencies?

  Moderator:

   Mr. Almir Colan, Director of AusCif, Director at Awqaf Australia, Careers Counsellor at 
Ilim College (Melbourne) and Board member at Garden College (Adelaide)

AAOIFI, established in 1991, and based in the Kingdom of Bahrain, is an international not-for-proﬁt organization primarily responsible 
for the development and issuance of standards for the global Islamic ﬁnance industry. These standards have been developed by a team 
of leading Shari’ah scholars, regulators, professionals, and researchers and which are recognized by 43* regulatory and supervisory 
authorities (RSAs) in 36* countries.

* As of October 2021

CPFAS is an advanced level certiﬁcate program targeted at Islamic ﬁnance accountancy professionals who want to develop correct 
understanding, interpretation, and implementation skills in the requirements of the AAOIFI ﬁnancial accounting standards. The 
certiﬁcate places greater emphasis on building technical / practical knowledge and skills in the real-world application of FASs at 
Islamic ﬁnancial institutions (IFIs) and for Islamic ﬁnance transactions. 

Why pursue the CPFAS certificate?
CPFAS not only oﬀers powerful learning content but also oﬀers strong career prospects.

1st AAOIFI roundtable on 

Issues and challenges for Islamic finance in adopting alternative benchmark rates and 
finding an Islamic benchmark rate (virtual) 

Tuesday, 18 January 2022 (Jumada II 15, 1443 AH)  

Agenda 

Since the recommendation of Financial Stability Board in 2014, a lot of work has been 
undertaken by financial regulators and other stakeholders to make reforms in the 
benchmark interest rates. These reforms are generally resulting in the replacement of IBOR 
based benchmark rates with risk-free rates (RFRs).  

Islamic Financial Institutions (IFIs) are different from their conventional counterparts in 
many aspects. To uphold the trust and interest of various stakeholders, it is a must that the 
transactions carried out by IFIs remain compliant with Shari’ah principles and rules. Shari’ah 
scholars have, in the past, allowed the use of conventional benchmarks for pricing of the 
Shari’ah compliant products. However, need has always been felt to have independent 
benchmark profit rates for the Islamic finance industry. This has become more important in 
view of the methodology of application of the RFRs in the financing contracts, which is 
generally on a retrospective basis. 

In this regard, AAOIFI has initiated multiple projects including developing a governance 
standard on alternative benchmark rates, in collaboration with other Islamic finance 
infrastructure institutions. This is the first in a series of roundtables on the subject, which 
aims to discuss and deliberate on the issue and its implications on the Islamic finance 
industry from Shari’ah, regulatory, legal, accounting, and governance perspectives.

Finance: The case of Crypto-Currency and 
Regulation 

By Dr. Mohammed Burhan Arbouna 

Group Head, Shari'a Compliance and Execution 

Al-Salam Bank- Bahrain 

AAOIFI – World Bank Annual Conference on Islamic Banking and 
Finance entitled “The Role of Standardization for the Future of Islamic 
finance, held on 23-24 October, 2018 at Diplomat Radisson, Manama, 
Kingdom of Bahrain   

1 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
1. Introduction 

It is a given fact that as part of human existence new developments and 
innovations are continuously emerging. It is unstoppable phenomenon 
which  all  scholars  and  thinkers  of  all  sects  and  religions  of  the  world 
admit. Therefore, it is not a surprise to note that the Muslim jurists are 
far ahead in this direction, because they had established a whole set of 
science  called  the  jurisprudence  of  innovations  and  emerging  issues, 
termed  as  fiqh  al-nawazil.  The  reason  being  that  scholars  admit  that 
texts  of  Quran  and  hadiths  of  the  Prophet,  peace  be  upon  him,  are 
limited while innovations are continuously emerging. This necessitates 
some  form  of  textual  interpretations  to  match  the  new  developments 
and innovations.  

In recent years, the world witnessed various developments which were 
not in the years back thinkable. Among these is the financial technology 
or  fintech  which  took  the  world  by  surprise  and  start  reshaping  the 
financial  system  and  the  way  businesses  are  conducted.  These 
developments forced intellectuals and scholars to open their minds and 
ponder on the impact of these developments on our current world and 
rules necessary to put things into perspective.  

Therefore,  this  conference  is  part  and  parcel  of  the  ongoing  efforts  in 
this direction. The conference requires discussion of an important topic 
which  is  related  to  public  as  whole  and  sovereign  state  vis-à-vis  its 
liability  to  protect  public  interest.  Its  importance  is  also  manifested  in 
the  fact  that  there  are  a  number  of  technical,  social,  legal  and  Sharia 
issues  surrounding  the  use  of  crypto-currency,  which  include,  among 
others,  the  possibility  of  regarding  something  that  is  not  feasible  or 
seen or cannot be touched as having the same qualities, characteristics 

2 

 
 
 
and duties of an intrinsic currency, i.e. currency by nature or by law. In 
this  context,  this  paper  is  trying  to  analyse  the  approaches  needed  in 
cases  of  innovations,  such  as  the  one  under  discussion:  the  crypto-
currency.  In  doing  so,  the  paper  concentrate  on  the  approach  of  fiqh 
literature to the nawazil.  

2. The Prediction and Current Reality Fiqh and Innovation  

As  noted  earlier,  the  Islamic  jurisprudence  is  a  science  of  innovation. 
The Muslim jurists in many instances are ahead of  certain happenings 
where they give views on them before their occurrences. For example, 
the  scholars  discussed  rule  of  praying  in  the  sky  before  airplanes  are 
invented.  So,  the  Islamic  literature  is,  in  fact,  a  literature  that 
encompasses various rules of tackling emerging issues and innovations.  

As this is the norm, the scholars were very cautious to open discussions 
on innovations without putting in place certain important parameters. 
In  this  context,  they  debated  extensively  on  whether  or  not  a  scholar 
should indulge in giving views on issues that did not happened yet. On 
this,  there  are  two  views,  but  the  prevalent  one  is  that  a  scholar  is 
allowed to give views on questions which subject matter did not occur. 
This approach created what is known as prediction fiqh, which is a sub-
category  of  innovation  fiqh,  or  fiqh  al-nawazil.  Hence,  the  new 
happenings are discussed extensively under  fiqh al-nawazil, which is a 
branch of fiqh having its own principle known to and applied by Islamic 
jurisprudence  experts  based  on  well-established  principles  far  from 
subjectivity, keeping in mind the general objectives of Sharia.  

3 

 
 
 
 
 
3. The Obligation of the Society to Request for the Ruling of 
Innovations  

A topic close to prediction fiqh is the call of scholars that at a time any 
emerging  issue,  the  uninformed  individuals  of  the  ruling  of  such 
innovation should endevour to seek the ruling from those who are well 
versed,  the  people  of  knowledge,  ahl  al-dhikr  who  knows  the  central 
rulings  of  new  cases  due  to  their  educational  training  and  thinking 
process on emerging issues.  Therefore, the principle in Islam is that the 
ummah  should  appeal  to  scholars  for  a  ruling  and  view  on  any  new 
development.  

This  requirement  is  meant  either  to  protect  society  from  evils  of 
innovations  or  lead  the  public  to  benefit  from  innovations.    And  the 
people  who  help  in  this  direction  are  the  pious  scholars  who  are 
preserved to study Shari’a and render rulings for new innovations and 
emerging  human  developments.  It  is  not  acceptable  for  any  given 
society  to  let  such  innovations  in  operation  without  knowing  their 
rulings.  Therefore,  it  is  not  surprising  that  we  see  scholars  today 
analyzing  Islamic  principles  applicable  to  innovations,  such  as  crypto-
currencies.  

In  brief,  Islamic  principles  of  innovation  emphasize  on  the  need  to 
involve scholars to determine the rulings of innovations before they are 
put into implementation.  

4. The Ways of Arriving at the Ruling of the Innovation 

4 

 
 
 
 
 
 
The  texts  of  the  Islamic  jurisprudence  are  either  directly  or  clearly 
pointing  at  the  ruling  of  a  new  case  which  is  call,  nass  or  indirectly 
through  dalala  or  indirect  understanding  which  amounts  to  qiyas.  In 
order  to  exercise  qiyas,  one  needs  to  look  at  the  illah  of  the  text 
applicable  to  new  cases  and  innovations.  Where  the  illahs  are  the 
same,  the  rulings  would  become  identical  hence  permissible  or  non-
permissible,  as  the  case  may  be.  In  the  recent  years,  a  technological 
innovation emerged which amount to among others the advent of what 
is  termed  as  crypto-currency;  which  is  a  form  of  non-touchable 
currency. This currency is in the form of numbers and codes in a digital 
container which is exchangeable with real world assets and currencies 
of government backing and protection. This form of innovation creates 
together with others the so-called smart transactions that need not the 
interference of lawyers or human beings to trigger their effect.  For all 
these,  there  is  a  dire  need  to  compare  this  innovation  with  relevant 
rules of currency and come up with a conclusion.  

5. The  scholarly and Regulatory Obligation of Public Protection and 
Innovation  

In  Sharia,  there  is  a  principle  called  dar  al-mafasid  muqaddamun  ala 
jalb  al-masalih,  i.e.  obviating  tribulations  prevails  over  generating 
advantages.  This  principle  is  the  cornerstone  for  the  regulators  and 
state  official  in  Islamic  law  for  managing  emerging  and  innovative 
issues.  This  is  because  its  application  protects  societies  against  any 
misconduct  and  ill-practices.  Therefore,  regulators,  state  officials  and 
scholars  are  required  to  give  more  weight  or  priority  to  addressing  or 
confronting  the  evils  of  any  innovation.  This  is  because  the  benefits 
anticipated  from  the  emerging  issues  shall  not  survive  in  the  face  of 

5 

 
 
 
 
uncontrolled evils. If evils are allowed even in a small quantity they will, 
at end of the day, overshadow the benefits anticipated from innovation 
and become prevailing. In other words, the fight against evils is actually 
a protection and development of the innovation. For this reason, in any 
innovation  the  scholars  take  a  stand  from  the  perspective  of  its  evils 
even  if  it  is  trivial  because  evil  is  like  a  fire  in  a  green  bush.  It 
immediately makes it dried and subject to burning quicker and faster.  

6.  Modern  Scholars  Approach  to  Crypto-currency  as  Medium  of 
Exchange    

It  is  no  doubt  that  the  issue  of  crypto-currency  is  among  the  greatest 
innovation which had taken scholars by surprise and its discussion had 
become a hot issue due to its immediate possible impact on people and 
their  economic  activities.  This  attention  is  also  driven  by  the  fact  that 
regulators and state institutions are trying to identify whether it can be 
used  as  a  tool  for  economic  criminality.  In  this  context,  the  scholars 
have different approaches and views on the reality and ruling of crypto-
currency and whether or not it meets the requirements and established 
Sharia principles of currency.   

When  the  issue  emerged,  a  number  of  scholars  opted  for  cautious 
approach  or  belated  voicing  of  views  until  all  aspects  of  the  crypto-
currencies are clear as the issue is not only to say it is a currency or not, 
but  rather  there  are  a  number  of  aspects  associated  with  it  that  may 
lead  to  declaring  it  as  a  currency  or  not.    This  approach  has  its 
advocates  in  the  literature  of  Islamic  law,  an  approach  which  is 

6 

 
 
 
 
 
sometimes  genuine  and  commendable  and,  in  some  cases,  it  is  a  tool 
for running away from real issues and non-desire to confront them.

AAOIFI  secretariat,  with  due  approval  of  the  respective  technical  boards,  in  April  2015  initiated  a 
comprehensive project on Sukuk which included plans to revise its existing Shari’ah standard on Sukuk 
(Shari’ah Standard No. 17) (issued 2003) and its Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) 25 “Investment 
in Sukuk, Shares, and Similar Instruments (issued 2010). This project aimed to reflect latest market 
developments with regards to Sukuk, particularly that the existing standards were issued relatively 
long time back. On the accounting side, the idea was to include other stakeholders as well, particularly 
the issuer or originator of the Sukuk for which accounting was earlier not defined. At a later stage, a 
governance standard was also added to the comprehensive project. 

During the discussions at various working group meetings as well as market interaction including a 
workshop for industry participants conducted for the project in October 2016, the need was eminent 
for accounting and financial reporting requirements for the benefit of the Sukuk-holders and other 
relevant stakeholders with regard to the assets and businesses underlying the Sukuk. This becomes 
more relevant for the instruments which are off-balance sheet for the principal issuer of Sukuk. 

This standard intends to introduce the financial reporting for the benefit of the Sukuk-holders and 
other stakeholders with regard to the assets and businesses underlying the Sukuk. It is expected that 
with the introduction of the same, the Sukuk market will have an improved level of transparency and 
financial discipline, as well as, enhance the level of compliance with Shari’ah will also.

Introduction 

Overview 

IN1 

IN2 

IN3 

This standard, AAOIFI Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) 34 “Financial Reporting for Sukuk-holders” 
intends to prescribe the accounting principles and reporting requirements for underlying assets of a 
Sukuk instrument. It requires the originator to prepare or cause to prepare the financing reports as 
needed under this standard. 

This  standard  prescribes  the  necessity  of  financial  reporting  for  the  benefit  of  Sukuk  instruments 
stakeholders’ and their underlying assets in a manner that a transparent and fair financial information 
sharing takes place. At the same time it does not intend to require comprehensive financial statements 
for the same and accordingly, allows a simplified version of financial reporting.  

This standard considers two primary types of Sukuk instruments for the purpose of financial reporting 
to the Sukuk-holders. Even within the two types of summarized financial information, it allows further 
simplified financial reporting for assets Sukuk, as compared to the business Sukuk considering the fact 
that the information needs for the stakeholders of such Sukuk are further limited. 

Rationale for issuing this standard 

IN4 

IN5

practices  of  financial  reporting  to  the  Sukuk-holders  or  for  the  accounting  for  the  SPVs.  Through 
market  interactions  and  expert  discussions  during  the  comprehensive  Sukuk  project,  the  need  for 
improved transparency and financial reporting practices was identified and noted by the Board.  

Accordingly,  the  Board  concluded  that  a  financial  reporting  standard  introducing  the  financial

INDONESIA - IFAAS 

NATIONAL SHARIAH BOARD OF INDONESIA – CASE STUDY 


The  National  Shariah  Board  or  Diwan  Syariah  Nasional  (“DSN-MUI”)  is  the  supreme 
authority  in  the  country  to  oversee  Shariah  compliance,  approve  Islamic  financial 
products and provide guidance, opinions and Fatwas related to Islamic finance matters 
 DSN-MUI  is  completely  independent  of  all  government  entities  and  part  of  the 







Indonesian Council of Scholars (Majlis Ulama Indonesia) 
The DSN-MUI consists of 35 scholars from different regions of the country representing 
different religious groups in Indonesia 
The  DSN-MUI  has  played  an  instrumental  role  in  the  development  and  growth  of  the 
Islamic financial industry in Indonesia. It has maintained its independent character within 
a  unique  model  of  Shariah  governance.  However,  the  current  structure  has  caused 
challenges for the market hence there is a need to redefine DSN-MUI’s role and enhance 
the overall framework of Shariah governance in the country  
The Islamic Finance Masterplan, recently launched by the Indonesian government, and 
prepared by IFAAS, includes a comprehensive set of recommendations in relation to DSN-
MUI that are very relevant to the context of CSBs across the world  

KEY RECOMMENDATIONS – SHARIAH GOVERNANCE 1/2 

Issue the general frameworks and guidelines instead of product approvals  

Number of scholars to be reduced to 7 for a term of 3 years with maximum 2 terms 

Scholars not able to sit simultaneously on DSN-MUI and any institutional SSBs 

Aggregate number of appointments (SSB, academic, pastoral etc) limited  

DSN-MUI to be subject to SLAs with Indonesian regulators 

Government allocation of annual budget for DSN-MUI (no other funding allowed) 

DSN-MUI plays a thought-leadership role in research and innovation 

Introduce a Fit & Proper criteria for all scholars at all levels including CPD requirements  

Enhancing internal audit and introducing external Shariah audit 

KEY RECOMMENDATIONS – SHARIAH GOVERNANCE 2/2 


Instate a Shariah Supervisory Board (“SSB”) in the Indonesian financial services authority 
or  Otoritas  Jasa  Keuangan  (“OJK”)  that  should  be  independent  and  work  in  accordance 
with the frameworks and guidelines set out by DSN-MUI 

 OJK  SSB  should  be  responsible  for  all  product  approvals  and  provide  guidance  to  the 

institutional Shariah boards 

 OJK SSB should seek guidance from the DSN-MUI in case of any issue related to Shariah 


The number of scholars on OJK SSB should not exceed 5 with a term in the office for 3 
years  renewable  once  only  (not  to  be  members  of  the  DSN-MUI  and  any  institutional 
SSBs at the same time)

Introduction, summary of proposals and invitation to comment 

Background 
IN1 

In 1993, the AAOIFI Financial Accounting Standards Board’s (AAB/the board) field study revealed 
inconsistent accounting practices for the widely used Murabaha transactions in Islamic finance. To 
address this, the board issued Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) 2, "Murabaha and Murabaha to 
the Purchase Orderer" in February 1996.  

IN2 

IN3 

In September 2017, the board decided to revise FAS 2 "Murabaha and Murabaha to the Purchase 
Orderer" to include deferred payment sales (i.e., those within the scope of FAS 20 “Deferred 
Payment Sale”) alongside Murabaha and align the accounting treatments with best practices. This 
resulted in issuance of FAS 28 “Murabaha and Other Deferred Payment Sales”.  

In the later stages of the revision project, the board decided to exclude Tawarruq and commodity 
Murabaha from the scope of FAS 28 "Murabaha and Other Deferred Payment Sales". The board 
noted that the Shari’ah standard on Tawarruq permits such transactions only as a last resort and 
discourages their widespread commercial use. The board concluded that the accounting and 
reporting for Tawarruq and commodity Murabaha requires separate consideration and decided to 
address it as a separate project in the future. 

IN4 

The board discussed Tawarruq and commodity Murabaha during several subsequent meetings. The 
board observed that: 

a. 

b. 

many Islamic financial institutions use Tawarruq as their primary instrument for liquidity 
management, a practice that is also endorsed by regulators, irrespective of AAOIFI’s view of 
discouraging its widespread usage; 

 the utilisation of Tawarruq in structuring Hybrid Sukuk is on the rise, with these instruments 
being issued more frequently across several markets. 

IN5 

IN6 

The board further noted different approaches, and challenges, in terms of current market practices. 
For example, certain users refer to FAS 28 “Murabaha and Other Deferred Payment Sales” in 
accordance with the “AAOIFI Conceptual Framework for Financial Reporting” as there is no specific 
standard for Tawarruq and Commodity Murabaha (considering the hierarchy of adoption and 
application of accounting policies, whereby the FAS on similar matters shall be consulted). However, 
FAS 28 “Murabaha and Other Deferred Payment Sales” specifically excludes Tawarruq, and the 
treatment of Tawarruq on the liability side my not suit all institutions and all situations. Other users 
apply generally accepted accounting principles to Tawarruq and Commodity Murabaha transactions. 
This leads to inconsistency in industry practices, as well as, accounting treatments are not aligned 
with the overall approach of AAOIFI FASs or the framework.  

Taking all these matters into account, the board decided to include Tawarruq and commodity 
Murabaha in its plan for development of a specific FAS on the matter. However, considering the 
complexity of the issues and practical challenges, after the stages of development of the preliminary 
study, consultation notes and the initial draft of the proposed standard, the board decided to have a 
mass scale stakeholders interaction before moving ahead. Accordingly, the board decided to publish 
this FAS-DP on the subject. 

4 

 
The business and Shari’ah compliance challenges 
IN7 

The primary business challenge is to ensure that Tawarruq and Commodity Murabaha transactions 
comply with the Shari’ah principles and rules. This has two particular aspects, i.e.: 

a. 

b. 

conducting the transaction as a genuine trade (through an independent four-party 
transaction) rather than disguising a loan through an elaborate structure; and 

utilising Tawarruq only in the case of necessity in line with the specific requirements and 
guidance provided by the respective Shari’ah Standard (SS)1. 

IN8 

Institutions, in general, find it challenging to manage liquidity through other means due to the lack 
of clear guidance, if resorting to Tawarruq is not possible or practicable. 

The accounting challenges 
IN9 

As a trading transaction, or rather a combination of multiple trading transactions, there are different 
risk profiles of Tawarruq and commodity Murabaha transactions at different stages for different 
parties, including the risks related to the underlying assets. Such risks shall be borne by the relevant 
parties and clearly reflected in their respective financial reporting for the benefit of users. 

IN10 

IN11

Conference sessions 

Session 1: Integrating AI in Islamic finance operations and services 

Session 2: Role of Islamic finance in an AI-driven business and finance environment 

Session 3: Islamic financial literacy and awareness: going beyond capacity-building 

Session 4: Entrepreneurship development through Shari’ah-compliant MSME financing 

Session 5: Salam and Istisna as tools for economic growth: practicality and accounting challenges 

Highlights of thematic sessions 

Integrating AI in Islamic finance operations and services 

•  AI identified as a key driver of operational efficiency and innovation. 
•  Enhanced potential for credit evaluation, fraud detection and risk management. 

Islamic finance in an AI-driven business environment 

•  AI expected to reshape global financial and business ecosystems. 
•  Ethical imperatives: fairness, transparency and accountability under Maqasid al-Shari’ah. 

Islamic financial literacy and awareness 

•  Capacity-building alone is inadequate for future industry needs. 
•  Public-level Islamic financial literacy identified as a major gap. 

MSME development through Shari’ah-compliant financing 

•  MSMEs remain underserved despite their economic contribution. 
•  Shari’ah-compliant financing tools offer viable alternatives to conventional credit. 

Salam and Istisna — Practicality, market reality and accounting challenges 
•  Salam and Istisna remain essential real-sector financing contracts. 
•  Significant use in trade, agriculture, infrastructure and export financing. 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
20th AAOIFI-IsDB Annual Islamic Banking and Finance Conference 2025 
2-3 November 2025, Kingdom of Bahrain 

Conference Overview 

The 20th AAOIFI-IsDB Islamic Banking and Finance Conference, convened on 2-3 November 2025 in the Kingdom 
of Bahrain, brought together more than 700 in-person and over 800 virtual participants from across the globe. 
The conference gathered leading industry experts, Shari’ah scholars, regulators, policymakers, academicians, and 
international service providers to deliberate on a wide range of pertinent topics aligned with the theme “Islamic Finance 
in the Era of Artificial Intelligence (AI): Present Potentials and Future Prospects.” 

This edition witnessed high-level international participation, including a delegation from the Republic of Tatarstan, 
Russian Federation, led by H.E. Mr. Midkhat Shagiakhmetov, deputy prime minister and minister of economy. The 
conference also benefited from the strong support and cooperation of the Islamic Development Bank through the 
Islamic Development Bank Institute (IsDBI), under the leadership of its Director General, H.E. Dr. Sami Al-Suwailem, as 
well as the active participation of the United Nations Industrial Development Organization (UNIDO). 

Senior representatives from around 25 regulatory and supervisory authorities worldwide took part in the conference, 
such as the Bank of Russia, Central Bank of Iraq, State Bank of Pakistan, Banque du Liban, Central Bank of Syria, Central 
Bank of Nigeria, Securities Commission of Nigeria, Central Bank of Somalia, Securities and Exchange Commission of 
Pakistan, Palestinian Monetary Authority, Bank of Tanzania, Central Bank of Uzbekistan, Financial Regulatory Authority 
of Oman, Bangladesh Bank, Indonesian Accounting Authority, Central Bank of Kosovo, Agency for the Republic of 
Kazakhstan for Regulation and Development of the Financial Market and the Central Bank of Oman. 

The flagship conference was held alongside the AAOIFI Capacity-Building Week (CBW) 2025 (3rd edition), which 
attracted more than 120 trainees from across the globe, further reinforcing AAOIFI’s mandate in professional 
development and standardisation. 

The conference addressed critical global economic developments reshaping the Islamic finance industry, with particular 
focus on artificial intelligence and its implications for business operations, Shari’ah compliance and the mobilisation of 
human and technological resources. It also underscored the importance of practical understanding of Islamic finance for 
empowering individuals and professionals to integrate ethical principles into daily operations and decision-making, while 
highlighting the value proposition of Islamic finance for micro, small, and medium-sized enterprises (MSMEs). 

Day-one proceedings 

Theme: Islamic finance in the era of artificial intelligence: Present potentials and future prospects 

Date: Sunday, 2 November 2025 

Venue: Crowne Plaza Hotel, Kingdom of Bahrain 

Opening ceremony 

- Recitation from the Holy Qur’an 

- Conference host’s keynote speech 

- H.E. Shaikh Ebrahim bin Khalifa Al Khalifa 
Chairman, AAOIFI Board of Trustees, Kingdom of Bahrain 

Shaikh Ebrahim bin Khalifa Al Khalifa delivered the host’s keynote address, 
emphasising that artificial intelligence is no longer a future concept but 
a present reality reshaping economies, institutions and societies. He 
highlighted projected global AI spending of USD 1.5 trillion by 2025 and its 
potential contribution of nearly USD 16 trillion to global GDP by 2030. While 
acknowledging AI’s transformative potential in efficiency, inclusion and 
value creation, he cautioned against ethical risks including job displacement, erosion of privacy and social exclusion. He 
stressed that Islamic finance must lead a values-driven approach to AI by embedding ethics, Maqasid al-Shari’ah and 
human dignity into technological advancement. 

Special address 

- H.E. Mr. Midkhat Shagiakhmetov 
Deputy Prime Minister and Minister of Economy, Republic of Tatarstan, 
Russian Federation 

H.E. Mr. Midkhat Shagiakhmetov highlighted the long-standing and evolving 
partnership between the Russian Federation and the Islamic world. He 
emphasised Tatarstan’s role as a bridge between Russia and Muslim 
countries, noting cooperation in trade, finance, energy, agriculture and 
technology. He underlined the importance of platforms such as the Kazan 
Economic Forum in strengthening economic and cultural ties and invited 
participants to future engagements aimed at deepening collaboration in Islamic finance and related sectors. 

Conference co-host’s keynote speech 

- Dr. Sami Al-Suwailem 
Acting Director General, Islamic Development Bank Institute 

Dr. Sami Al-Suwailem underscored the role of technology in enhancing 
the stability, resilience and productivity of Islamic finance. Drawing on 
economic research and insights from Nobel laureate Joseph Stiglitz, he 
discussed the high social costs of debt financing and bankruptcy, noting that 
corporate bankruptcies impose hidden burdens on society. He emphasized 
that Islamic finance, supported by ethical technology adoption, offers a 
more resilient alternative through risk-sharing and real-economy linkages. 

Welcome address 

- Mr. Omar Mustafa Ansari 
Secretary General, AAOIFI 

Mr. Omar Mustafa Ansari welcomed participants and highlighted the strong 
confidence placed in AAOIFI by the global Islamic finance industry, reflected 
in nearly 1,200 physical registrations. He provided an update on AAOIFI’s 
achievements, including the issuance of 127 standards to date, with nine 
additional standards expected by year-end. He expressed gratitude to the 
Central Bank of Bahrain, the Islamic Development Bank Group, and the 
more than 450 experts from over 40 countries contributing voluntarily to AAOIFI’s standard-setting work. 

Token of appreciation and agreements ceremony 

A ceremony was held to recognise guests of honour, keynote speakers and conference sponsors for their contributions 
and support. Distinguished representatives from regulatory authorities, financial institutions and partner organizations 
were acknowledged. 

Session 1: Integrating AI in Islamic finance operations and services 

This session explored the application of artificial intelligence in Islamic 
finance operations and service delivery. Panelists discussed the use of 
AI in credit evaluation, risk management, fraud detection, customer 
interaction and Shari’ah compliance. The discussion emphasised cost 
efficiency, operational effectiveness, and the challenges of adopting AI while 
maintaining ethical and Shari’ah standards. 

- Islamic finance success stories: Learning from the leaders 
- Case study: Meezan Bank Limited 

The success story highlighted the journey of Meezan Bank, founded in 1992 
as an Islamic investment bank and later transformed into Pakistan’s first 
full-fledged Islamic commercial bank. The presentation traced its growth 
from modest beginnings to commanding over one-third of Pakistan’s Islamic 
banking market. Emphasis was placed on strong governance, Shari’ah 
compliance, cost efficiency, ethical values and long-term vision as key 
drivers of success. 

Session 2: Role of Islamic finance in an AI-Driven business and finance 
environment 

This session examined how artificial intelligence is reshaping global business 
and finance and its implications for Islamic finance. Discussions addressed 
ethical considerations, employment impacts, financial inclusion, smart 
contracts, and AI-driven decision-making. Practical experiences from Islamic

Introduction
With growing support and interest in Islamic / participation ﬁnance sector both from private and public institutions, 
Turkey is becoming a vital player in the global Islamic ﬁnance sector. The share of participation banking industry in Turkey 
has surpassed 7% of the banking industry, while the government-driven strategic target is to increase the share to 15% by 
2025. In this regard, Turkey has in recent years introduced host of new initiatives, including initiating legal and regulatory 
infrastructure development in banking, insurance, capital markets, stepping up its capacity building activities and 
intellectual capital, as well as, establishing new institutions and departments in existing institutions etc. The regulatory 
and supervisory institutions like BDDK, KGK together with the Central Bank of Turkey and the oﬃce of H.E. the President 
are playing a leadership role in the development of the Islamic / participation ﬁnance industry in the country.
Additionally, international infrastructure institutions such as AAOIFI and IIRA with support of TKBB have been providing 
the much-needed support and guidance in terms of advocacy, standards-setting and capacity building to accelerate the 
development of the Islamic / participation ﬁnance sector in Turkey. 
This webinar will bring together important stakeholders from Turkey and global industry to boost the Islamic / 
participation ﬁnance sector in Turkey and deliberate on opportunities and recent initiatives to support further 
development of the sector in the country.  The event will also shed light on the latest updates and initiatives that are 
taking place at regulatory and supervisory front, as well, as how the AAOIFI standards can further provide guidance to 
ensure a robust sector is developed.

3 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Preface 

PR1 

The objective of the standard on Wa’ad, Khiyar and Tahawwut is to set out the accounting principles  
for measurement, recognition and disclosure of Wa’ad, Khiyar and Tahawwut transactions that are 
carried out by Islamic financial institutions (IFIs).  

PR2  Wa’ad and Khiyar are used by IFIs in various modes. Some of these modes are ancillary to other 

transactions whereas in other products these are used as a primary product. This standard intends to 
provide accounting principles for both of these situations, as well as, the Tahawwut (hedging) 
transactions which are normally based on Wa’ad or Khiyar, or a series or combination thereof. These 
may be converted into products like a series of commodity Murabaha transactions, as well.   

4 

 
 
 
 
 
Introduction 

Overview 

IN1 

This Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) No. ___ “Wa’ad, Khiyar and Tahawwut” prescribes the 
accounting and reporting principles and requirements for Wa’ad (promise), Khiyar (option) and 
Tahawwut (hedging) arrangements for IFIs. 

IN2  Many products e.g. Murabaha, Ijarah, diminishing Musharaka etc. offered by IFIs combine application 

of Wa'ad or Khiyar in one form or the other. Ancillary Wa’ad or Khiyar, in line with this standard, is 
such Wa’ad or Khiyar which is associated with an Islamic finance arrangement by relation to its 
structure which does not give rise to any asset or liability unless it has turned into an onerous contract 
or commitment.  

IN3 

Product Wa’ad or Khiyar, on the other hand, is a stand-alone Islamic finance arrangement which is 
used either as a normal product or at times for the purpose of Tahawwut (hedging). It may take form 
of a single transaction or a series or combination thereof, and may convert into a future transaction or 
series of transactions, in line with Shari’ah principles. Such transactions give rise to an asset or a 
liability for the parties, subject to the conditions specified in this standard. 

Rationale for issuing this standard 

IN4 

AAOIFI Accounting Board (the Board) included the Wa’ad and Khiyar project in its long-term work plan 
as these are integral part of many Shari’ah compliant products, and their use as a stand-alone product 
is also increasing with the development of industry. The Board felt that clear guidance is required to 
ensure that accounting treatment of Wa’ad and Khiyar are similar and comparable between 
jurisdictions and entities. During the project, it was decided to include the treatment of Tahawwut 
arrangements in the scope of this standard that are now increasingly gaining popularity due to 
acceptance of elaborate risk management strategies by IFIs.

Objective of the Standard 

1. 

The objective of this standard is to prescribe the appropriate accounting and reporting principles for 
recognition, measurement and disclosures in relation to Shari'ah compliant Wa’ad (promise), Khiyar 
(option) and Tahawwut (hedging) arrangements for Islamic financial institutions (IFIs). 

Scope 

2. 

This standard applies to accounting and financial reporting for all transactions involving Wa’ad, Khiyar 
or Tahawwut arrangements carried out under Shari’ah principles and rules, as provided in this 
standard.

AAOIFI 

Newsletter 

1 July – 31 December 2025 | Vol. 7, Issue 2 

From the desk
of the Secretary General 

Greetings from AAOIFI! 

It is my privilege to present this edition of the AAOIFI Newsletter, highlighting the key 
achievements, milestones, and strategic initiatives undertaken during the second half 
of 2025. This publication reflects AAOIFI’s continued commitment to maintaining 
transparent communication with its valued stakeholders and keeping them informed of 
progress across its standard-setting activities, institutional developments, international 
cooperation, and advocacy efforts aimed at advancing the global Islamic finance 
industry. 

The period under review witnessed the successful convening of our flagship 20th 
AAOIFI-IsDB Islamic Banking and Finance Conference, alongside notable 
accomplishments across its technical boards, namely the AAOIFI Shari’ah Board (ASB), 
AAOIFI Accounting Board (AAB), AAOIFI Governance and Ethics Board (AGEB) and the 
activities in the domain of capacity building, including in particular, the revamping of certification and fellowship 
programmes. Alhamdulillah, AAOIFI concluded the year with the number of its standards reaching a total of 131, 
reflecting the dedicated efforts of its esteemed board members, technical experts and supporting teams. 

Secretary General, AAOIFI 

A further significant milestone during this period was the commencement of a new phase in AAOIFI’s global 
engagement strategy through strengthened collaboration with regulators, academic institutions, and financial entities 
in emerging markets. This was marked by the inauguration of the Centre for Islamic Finance Development in Kazan, 
the capital of the Republic of Tatarstan in the Russian Federation, reinforcing AAOIFI’s expanding international 
presence. 

During the same period, AAOIFI enhanced its strategic positioning through the signing of additional memorandums of 
understanding (MoUs) with international organisations, expansion of its institutional membership base, and continued 
delivery of multilingual training programmes and professional certification initiatives across various jurisdictions to 
strengthen industry capacity. 

As AAOIFI reflects on these achievements, sincere appreciation is extended to the AAOIFI Board of Trustees (BoT), the 
Public Interest Monitoring Consultative Committee (PIMCC), the technical boards, institutional members and global 
partners for their steadfast support and contribution. Their collective commitment remains fundamental to AAOIFI’s 
sustained growth and impact. 

AAOIFI enters 2026 with renewed determination to further promote integrity, standardisation, governance excellence 
and Shari’ah compliance across the global Islamic finance industry, Insha’Allah.

On behalf of the Accounting and 
Auditing Organization for Islamic 
Financial Institutions (AAOIFI) and 
the Chairman of the AAOIFI Board 
of Trustees, H.E. Shaikh Ebrahim bin 
Khalifa Al Khalifa, it gives me immense 
pleasure to bring forth this issue of 
AAOIFI’s newsletter. 

Through this newsletter, we update our 
stakeholders on the activities of and 
developments at AAOIFI, in addition to sharing 
industry engagements and initiatives that we 
undertake for the benefit of the global Islamic 
finance industry. 

It has indeed been a challenging year for the 
humanity as a whole across the globe due 
to COVID-19 pandemic, which has wreaked 
havocs at health and economic fronts. The 
impact of it has been far and wide resulting in 
deaths of millions of people around the world 
while millions other are undergoing medical 
treatment. Additionally, the economic impact of 
the pandemic has left the economies shattered, 
where hundreds of businesses have been closed 
due to lock downs, curfews, etc. As a result, 
governments around the world took proactive

In January 2020, after an extensive selection 
process adopted by the AAOIFI nominations 
committee, the AAOIFI Board of Trustees 
announced the reconstitution of its three 
technical boards and appointed respective 
members for the next 4-year term. Upon 
reconstitution the technical boards approved 
the strategy for the next four years as well as 
the charter. The technical boards also approved 
and issued twelve standards and two statements 
during the year 2020; one from the accounting 
board and the other from the governance and 
ethics board, as guidance for institutions and 
jurisdictions adopting AAOIFI standards as well as 
3 exposure drafts.

The capacity building programs of AAOIFI 
remained on track, despite the restrictions in 
place as the exam centers in many countries were 
closed due to lockdowns and curfews, etc. Yet, 
more than a hundred professionals sat for the two 
qualifications, which AAOIFI offers. Additionally, 
virtual workshops were organised to ensure the 
learning and development on AAOIFI standards 
continues for colleagues around the world. On our 
advocacy and awareness front, AAOIFI organised 
four international virtual events during the year. 
These included an event on COVID-19 and its 
impact on Islamic finance, AAOIFI Shari’ah Boards 
Conference, AAOIFI Footprint launch event and 
AAOIFI-IsDB conference. AAOIFI also launched its 
inaugural AAOIFI Footprint Report 2020 with the 

help of more than 90 regulators around the world. 
Through this report it was reported that AAOIFI 
standards are fully / partially adopted / adapted  
and / or used as guidelines in more than 43 
jurisdictions globally. Lastly, AAOIFI also published 
two issues of its bi-annual academic publication 
Journal of Islamic Finance Accountancy (JOIFA). 

The year also witnessed the sad demise of the 
Deputy Chairman of the AAOIFI Board of Trustees 
– H.E. Sheikh Saleh Abdullah Kamel (founder 
of Dallah Al Barakah Group) and Haj Saeed Bin 
Ahmad Al Lootah (founder of Dubai Islamic 
Bank) as well as AAOIFI Shari’ah Board Members 
Sheikh Dr. Abdul Sattar Abu Ghudah and Sheikh 
Dr. Hussain Hamid Hassan. All these personalities 
played an instrumental role in the development 
of global Islamic banking and finance industry and 
have been strong supporters of AAOIFI. We pray 
to Allah SWT to accept their noble services and 
elevate their ranks in Jannah, Ameen. 

Overall, we would like to take this opportunity 
to thank all our stakeholders and look forward 
to your support in the years to come, especially 
in the post COVID-19 world. We pray to Allah 
SWT to lift this hardship from us and bless the 
institutions and individuals with strength to cope 
with these difficult times. With this, I look forward 
to addressing you again and providing an update 
on the developments at AAOIFI towards the end 
of H1 2021, Insha Allah.

AAOIFI developments
Standards development

Standards development is the core activity of AAOIFI and currently has 117 standards and technical 
pronouncements in issue. As you are aware, AAOIFI develops standards in five areas—Shariah, 
accounting, auditing, governance, and ethics — all of which, complement each other to form a 
bouquet of standards for Islamic finance stakeholders seeking to protect, enhance and stregnthen 
the integrity of the global Islamic finance industry.

Of the 117 standards and technical pronouncements in issue, 59 are Shari’ah standards, 33 are 
accounting standards, 8 are auditing standards, 14 are governance standards, and 3 are codes 
of ethics. These standards are developed and issued to achieve AAOIFI’s overall objectives to 
standardize and harmonize international Islamic finance practices and financial reporting, in 
accordance with Shari’ah principles and rules, so as to support growth and expansion of Islamic 
finance industry globally. 

AAOIFI standards are adopted in more than 40 regulatory jurisdictions either fully, partially 
or as guidelines / reference material. Consequently, the standards have introduced greater 
harmonization of Islamic finance practices across the world.

of developing and issuing standards during the year 2020.

Payment cards

A Shari’ah standard on the topic was issued more

financial instruments and transactions resulting

decided to revise this important standard.

discussed and deliberated in a series of meetings.

members of AAOIFI’s Shari’ah Board, with senior 
representatives of Visa company were also 
arranged, which enhanced the understanding 
about the technical operations of credit cards.

Islamic Agricultural Finance Products 

Murabaha – Cost Plus Sale 

Murabaha means a sale on mutually agreed profit, also known as cost plus sales, it 
is one of the most widely used instruments for short-term agricultural financing 
where the Bank/MFI undertakes to supply specific goods or commodities at 
mutually agreed profit to the client. 

Utilization : 

Murabahah can be utilize for Purchase of seed, fertilizer, harvesting and planting 
equipments, agri. Inputs, tractor, pesticides, farming goods, Solar Tube-wells  etc. 

Currently Practiced: 

MBL and some other Banks, NRSP – KPK,  Al Amal Bank, Islamic Bank Bangladesh, 

 FINCA-AF, Islamic Relief, BMT’s in Indonesia etc.  

 
 
Islamic Agricultural Finance Products 

Salam – Forward Sale 

Salam means a contract in which advance payment is made for goods to be 
delivered later on. The seller undertakes to supply some specific crop/goods 
to the buyer at a future date in exchange of an advance price fully paid at 
the time of contract  

Utilization 

Salam is ideal product for Agricultural Financing, through which a farmer can 
fulfill all the financial needs of whole crop circle e.g. liquidity, seed, 
pesticide, fertilizer, harvesting, irrigation, transportation, market linkages 
etc.   

Currently Practiced: 

Wasil Foundation, Albarakah MPCS, MBL etc.  

 
 
Islamic Agricultural Finance Products 

Istisna - Manufacturing contract 

A  pre-delivery financing instrument used to finance projects where 
commodity is transacted before it comes into existence. It is an order to 
manufacture and payment of price, unlike Salam, it’s flexible, where price 
may be paid in advance, or in installments or on delivery of good.  

Utilization: 
Istisna May Utilize for small manufacturing Business, dairy or Agri. 
production, Construction of wharehouses and cold storage, Rural 
entrepreneur Development etc. 

Currently Practiced: 
AlBaraka MPCS, Ghana Islamic Microfinance bank,  
Al-Amal Bank, Bank of Khartoum etc.  

 
 
 
 
Islamic Agricultural Finance Products 

Ijara – Islamic Lease 

Ijarah Means Operating lease. It is an arrangement in which the Islamic 
Bank lease Agri. equipments, Tractor & light vehicles, Agri. Instruments, 
buildings or other facilities to a client, against an agreed rental. 

Utilization : 

Leasing of Tractors, Agri. Equipment, Threshers, Tub wells, small production 

unit lease, Sugarcane planter, Rice planter, harvesting vehicles etc.    

Currently Practiced: 

Al-Amal Microfinance Bank, NRDP,  Wasil Foundation. 

Amanah Islamic Bank – Philippines.  

 
 
Islamic Agricultural Finance Products 

Diminishing Musharkah 

Diminishing Musharkah is a form of Musharakah where the Bank/MFI and 
the client participate in a joint commercial enterprise or property. This 
attains the form of undivided ownership of both, Over certain period the 
equity of financier is purchased by the client 

Utilization: 

It can be utilize for Rural housing, forest development, agri. Inputs, 
farming, sprinkler/drip/solar pumps, tube wells, Refrigeration vans  etc. 

Currently Practiced: 

Islamic Bank Bangladesh, some Islamic banks etc.  

 
 
 
Islamic Agricultural Finance Products 

Musharakah & Mudarabah- Partnership 

Musharakah & Mudaraba means a relationship established under a 
contract by the mutual consent of the parties for sharing of profits and 
losses in the joint business. 

Utilization : 

Musharkah and Mudarabah can be used for Microenterprise & SME’S 
setup’s, Agricultural Joint venture projects, Dairy and livestock 
development etc.  

Currently Practiced: 

BOK, Amanah Islamic bank, AlBarakah MPCS etc

Challenges for the Development of Islamic 
Agri. & Rural Finance 

 Non - Availability of Donor/Shariah Compliant Sources 

of Funds 

 Need to develop a uniform regulatory and legal 
framework for the Islamic Agricultural Finance. 

 Accounting & I.T systems., Rating Agencies.  
 Lack of Quality HR in Islamic Agri./Rural Finance Sector. 
 Standardization of Products. 
 Reluctance in Research & Implementation of  new 

Products, as only (Murabahah) is serving almost 80% of 
Islamic Agri./Microfinance Industry.  

 Introduce new product line on the basis of Musaqa, 

Muzara’a & Mugharasa 

 Development of Shairah Expertise towards the Growth 

of Islamic Agricultural Finance.

Agenda of AAOIFI – World Bank Annual Conference, Edition 14

“Revolutionary Changes in Global Islamic Finance Ecosystem: 
Need for Governance, Standardization
and Regulatory Support”
3 - 4 November 2019, Kingdom of Bahrain

The global financial industry has witnessed fluctuations and changes post the financial crisis which 
hit the world economies over a decade ago. The stability and growth of the industry has been affected 
by  various  factors  and  many  practitioners  are  of  the  view  that  there  is  a  dire  need  for  more  effective 
regulations,  prudential  rules,  and  stricter  polices  to  safeguard  the  industry  from  another  turmoil.  

The  recent  developments  in  FinTech,  including  but  not  limited  to  cryptocurrencies,  blockchain 
technology, smart contracts, crowd-funding and artificial intelligence introduce new risks to the whole 
industry. While the benefits of cost saving and efficiency appeal to many market players and new entrants 
alike,  these  technologies  require  effective  mechanisms  to  govern  their  applications.

While the Islamic finance industry is keen to keep pace with the recent developments, additional layers 
of challenges and obstacles exist in terms of Shari’ah and governance. The question is how can the players 
take the industry to the next level of development and growth without risking its stability in this dynamic 
age?

In this regard, the 14th edition of AAOIFI-World Bank Conference sheds light on the need for regulations, 
governance  and  standardization  in  the  industry  from  different  angles  through  the  lenses  of  a  host  of 
leading experts and scholars, representatives of central banks and regulatory and supervisory authorities, 
and practitioners.

   Session 1: Overcoming regulatory challenges posed by technology 
disruptions and technology-based unregulated financial intermediation

Capitalizing on the potential efficiencies, cost-savings, and new market opportunities created 
by low-margin disruptive technologies has become a new normal in today’s dynamic financial 
industry. As technology matures and gains a larger audience and market share, it threatens the 
status quo, finding a right combination of these will not only lift the Islamic finance industry to 
greater heights, but also will reinforce it’s position with the unique propositions it has to offer 
to the global financial industry.

A  right  mix  of  technology,  aided  by  prudent  regulatory  support  in  a  challenging 

macroeconomic  environment  will  hold  the  key  to  sustainable  success. 

Distinguished panellists will provide expert inputs to guide the Islamic finance industry to 

ride the technology wave and overcome challenges faced by the industry.

   Session 2: Blockchain-based smart contracts: the need for Shari’ah rules 
and governance mechanism

Blockchain-technology has emerged to save cost and increase efficiency by allowing untrusted 
entities to interact with each other  in  a  distributed  manner  without  the involvement  of  a  
trusted  third  party. An appealing application that can be deployed on top of blockchain is smart 
contracts. There has been an increasing trend towards the use of smart contracts which can be 
applied  in  areas  ranging  from  financial  services,  healthcare  to  energy  resources  and  voting. 
A  smart  contract  is  an executable  code  that  runs  on  top  of  the  blockchain  to  facilitate,  
execute  and  enforce  an  agreement  between  untrusted  parties  without the  involvement  of  
a trusted  third  party. However, since smart contracts are still in early stage of development, 
legal and regulatory challenges are there for the users and developers. This session will provide 
a broader perspective on the challenges and possible solutions.

  Session 3: Islamic crowdfunding: opportunities and challenges
Crowdfunding has become a popular alternative to traditional funding. Countless businesses 
have  started  and  thousands  of  jobs  created,  thanks  to  the  power  of  crowdfunding. As  such, 
growth  in  crowdfunding  and  peer  to  peer  lending  platforms  is  blooming  worldwide,  the 
industry is projected to grow to over $300 billion by 2025. Crowdfunding allowed people around 
the world to raise funds for various projects and causes. For the Islamic finance industry, it is 
a new development and has the potential to change the whole business model. Accordingly, it 
is crucial to shed light on opportunities and challenges it offers to Islamic finance. This session 
consists of 2 papers presented by industry experts. After the presentations the floor will be open 
for the audience for further discussions.

  Main Topics

   The role of crowdfunding in promoting the growth of the economy.
   How can crowdfunding help in financial inclusion?
   Impact of crowdfunding in enhancing the operational efficiency and lowering costs.
   Risks of fraud in crowdfunding and potential controls to avoid such risks.
   What are the possible governance gaps/ traps in crowdfunding?
   The need for clear rules governing and regulating the crowdfunding platforms.
   What are the mechanisms that should govern crowdfunding? 
   What are the implications of crowdfunding expansion on banks business models?

Preface 
PR1 

As Islamic finance industry continues to evolve in structure and complexity, it has become 
increasingly important to ensure that the accounting and financial reporting in respect of costs and 
expenses, and their recoverability / reimbursement, supports both reliable financial reporting and 
the proper application of Shari’ah principles and rules, in practice.  

PR2 

Through this Standard, AAOIFI Accounting Board (AAB / the board) seeks to reduce divergence in 
practice, mitigate the risk of misapplication or misinterpretation of Shari’ah principles and rules 
arising from accounting treatments, and support the Islamic financial institutions in achieving 
greater discipline and consistency in pricing, execution and reporting of Shari’ah-compliant 
transactions. The development of this standard reflects continuing commitment of the board to 
strengthen the integrity, transparency and consistency of financial reporting within the Islamic 
finance industry.  

4 

 
 
 
Introduction 

Overview 
IN1 

This standard entails the principles for the determination, attribution and reimbursement of direct 
costs (and expenses) incurred by Islamic financial institutions (the institutions / IFIs), in connection 
with Islamic finance transactions and other Shari’ah-compliant contracts and arrangements. It 
comprehensively addresses their respective recording, reporting, presentation and disclosure in a 
fair, transparent and Shari’ah-compliant manner.  

IN2 

These principles are not only necessary for the purpose of fair and transparent financial reporting, 
but also, at times, considered relevant for the purpose of ensuring Shari’ah compliance in the 
execution of certain Shari’ah-compliant transactions and their respective pricing / returns. 

Rationale for issuing this standard 
IN3 

AAOIFI Accounting Board (AAB / the board), based on certain stakeholders’ queries and comments 
received by the AAOIFI secretariat, identified that there is a need to clarify the accounting and 
financial reporting requirements for direct costs and expenses (corresponding with the relevant 
Shari’ah principles and rules applicable to different types of Islamic finance transactions and 
structures). It was noted that while the core principles with regard to attribution, recoverability, 
chargeability, etc., of direct costs and expenses, and their inclusion in the product pricing are 
included in the AAOIFI Shari’ah Standards, there is still a need to provide further guidance on the 
accounting and financial reporting aspects of the same.  

IN4 

It was also noted that absence of such guidance on the accounting side, in certain situations, may 
result in inappropriate interpretation and application of the relevant Shari’ah principles and rules, 
and hence, accounting guidance is also needed to improve the level of compliance with Shari’ah 
principles and rules, across the industry. 

IN5 

Accordingly, the board decided to prescribe all such requirements, at one place, in form of this 
standard.

Five-step model for income recognition

Identify the contract(s) with a customer that has been approved by the parties to the contract, 
containing each party's rights and obligations, the performance obligations and the payment 
terms for the goods and services

Identify the performance obligations in the contract at the inception of the contract that are 
distinct, or substantially the same, with a similar pattern of transfer

Determine the transaction price to which the institution expects to be entitled in exchange for 
goods and services

Allocate the transaction price to the performance obligations in the contract by reference of 
their stand-alone selling price or, where stand-alone price is not available, the estimated selling 
price

Recognize income when (or as) the institution satisfies a performance obligation and transfers 
the control, either at a point in time or over a period of time

English law as a governing law

English law as a choice of law in Islamic finance transactions (continuation).

English law and Courts of England and Wales have significantly contributed to the development of Islamic finance and its 
instruments at a very critical junction in the modern Islamic economy where enforcement of instruments is of paramount 
importance and resultantly, the Courts of England and Wales paved the way for reforms and adaptability of the principles 
of Sharia to become a unified body of law for the purpose of becoming a choice of law for Islamic finance transactions.

The  court’s  decisions  in  Shamil  Bank  case  (2004),  Bloom  Bank  case (2009)  and  Dana Gas  case  (2017)  have  not 
only subverted the attempt of a willful repudiation of the obligation in Islamic finance instrument but resulted in filling 
the gaps and further most need improvements in law, regulation, standards and practices in Islamic finance. 

The experience has set in motion the drive and race for regulatory reforms and standardization in the GCC, particularly in 
the UAE.

English law as a governing law

Does it matter – Sharia perspective. The ruling on making the English law (and the likes) as the 
applicable law for contracts and documentation of the Syndicated facilities and Sukuk, and other 
transactions involving international parties.

The core principles underpinning the English law (highlighted earlier in this presentation) and the approach of the Courts 
of England and Wales in the earlier reported judicial precedents lead us to the conclusion that the Courts of England and 
Wales may indirectly apply the principles of Sharia in commercial disputes between the parties and as such has been 
respectful to the principles of Sharia to the extent clearly identified and reflected in the contract between the parties.

It is the procedural law limb of English law and the platform (i.e. the Courts of England and Wales) that are relevant.

In the event there is no ambiguity or uncertainty in formulation of the contract between the parties in a Sharia compliant 
manner (based on a clearly black letter provisions of law, standards or set of rules chosen by the parties to govern their 
contractual relationship), subjecting the contract to English law will leave a limited scope for the court to disregard 
contractual consideration between the parties. *

“In various ways, and with varying degrees of success, English law permits contractual counterparties to frame their own 
legal relationship in a way which does pay due respect to the Sharia principles generally, and in particular to the 
prohibition of interest.” **

**

Application of principles of Sharia

Principles of Sharia as a governing law – Litmus Test

Under English law, the choice of law (the governing law of a contract) is limited to a choice of law between the specific law of 
countries or a foreign law of a country based on the principles of Sharia.

“The doctrine of incorporation can only sensibly operate where the parties have by the terms of their contract sufficiently identified 
specific ‘black letter’  provision of a foreign law or an international code or set of rules apt to be incorporated as terms of the 
relevant contract such as a particular article or articles of the French Civil Code or the Hague Rules.” *

“The general reference to principles of Sharia in this case affords no reference to, or identification of, those aspects of Sharia law 
which are intended to be incorporated into the contract, let alone the terms in which they are framed. It is plainly insufficient for 
the defendants to contend that the basic rules of the Sharia applicable in this case are not controversial. Such ‘basic rules’ are 
neither referred to nor identified. Thus, the reference to the “principles of … Sharia” stand unqualified as a reference to the body 
of Sharia law generally. As such, they are inevitably repugnant to the choice of English law as the law of the contract and render 
the clause self-contradictory and therefore meaningless.” **

*

project to its active agenda

losses

expected losses (”front loading 
of financing revenue”)

impairment/ estimates undermine

   Session 1: Panel discussion “AAOIFI Shari’ah Standards: the way 
forward”

  Main topics
   methodology of drafting / setting Sharia standards;
   comprehensiveness of Shari’ah standards in meeting requirements of the 
Islamic financial industry;
   review / revision / amendment methodology and process; 
   building blocks / components of Shari’ah standards;

   procedures for communication with regulatory authorities and industry 
institutions with regard to issuance of a new standard or amendment of an 
existing standard; and
   language of development process for new Shari’ah standards: Arabic vs. 
“simultaneous” Arabic and English languages

   Session 3: Commingling balances of investment accounts with funds of 
general portfolio in conventional banks that operate Islamic windows
  Main topics
   Shari’ah basis / ruling for commingling of investment account balances with 
funds of a conventional bank ;
   is it sufficient for the bank’s permissible finances and investments to be more 
than the investment account balances without separating their assets?;
   impact of non- compartmentalization of sources of funds on adjustment of 
investment account profits, attribution of losses, setting up of provisions, and 
allocation of expenses / costs;
   can non-compartmentalization be considered a form of Jahalah pertaining 
to Mudaraba funds, which may mandate guarantee by an Islamic bank in its 
capacity as Mudarib;
   does Shari’ah mandate compartmentalization for assets financed by investment 
accounts? Is compartmentalization “actual” or “only” in the books of accounts?; and
   can a conventional bank issue Tier 1 Capital Sukuk on the basis of Mudaraba, 
with compartmentalization or without?

AAOIFI’s online professional 
workshops 
2024 

Introduction 
AAOIFI is pleased to announce the annual training calendar for 2024, which marks the continuation of its specialised 
online workshops for the fourth consecutive year. These workshops are specifically designed to improve participants’ 
proficiency in correctly understanding, interpreting and applying AAOIFI standards. To date, AAOIFI has successfully 
conducted more than 40 workshops and plans to conduct 27 such workshops this year alone. 

AAOIFI workshops are designed and developed to cater to specific needs and requirements of the representatives of the 
regulatory and supervisory authorities, employees of Islamic financial institutions and other Islamic finance practitioners 
such as Shari’ah scholars and consultants, including AAOIFI fellows, students and researchers. 

The workshops will be facilitated by Secretary General of AAOIFI, members of the AAOIFI Shari'ah Board, AAOIFI 
Accounting Board and AAOIFI Governance and Ethics board, including members of the AAOIFI Education Board, in 
addition to other AAOIFI master and associate trainers, as well as technical staff of AAOIFI secretariat. 

In addition to providing continuous professional development (CPD) hours, each workshop will award attendees with an 
AAOIFI certificate of attendance.

Vision and mission 

Vision 

Mission 

To support the growth of the Islamic finance industry by guiding its 
operations on Shari’ah principles and rules through development of 
standards and guidelines. 

To serve the public interest by issuing international standards on Shari’ah, 
accounting, governance and ethics for Islamic finance industry. 

AAOIFI at a glance 

About

• AAOIFI stands for Accounting and Auditing Organization for Islamic Financial Institutions
• AAOIFI is a not-for-profit organisation

Organisational structure

• Established in 1991 by key industry stakeholders
• 188 active institutional members as at 31 December 2024
• Key organs include: General Assembly, Board of Trustees, Public Interest Monitoring Consultative 
Committee, three technical boards and secretariat

Primary objectives

• 127 standards and technical pronouncements in issue as at 1 January 2025 – 5 areas
• Shari’ah – 59
• Accounting – 36
• Auditing – 7
• Governance – 24
• Ethics – 1 

Recognition and accessibility

• Adopted, adapted or used as guidance in more than 47 regulatory jurisdictions
• Accessible in multiple languages

Secondary objectives

• Capacity building
• Advocacy and awareness
• Advisory and assurance services
• Research and development

meet its financial obligations as they fall due. 

What It Is 

And What It Is Not

• A guarantee of credit quality or default

What do we rate in Islamic finance?  

In Islamic finance, we rate:  

1- Islamic financial institutions and Takaful companies (using the same 
methodology we apply to conventional issuers but factoring the specificities 
related to their Islamic nature in our analysis). 

2- Sukuk: based on a specific methodology that was published initially in 
2007 and updated in 2015. Sharia compliance is not part of the factors that 
we incorporate in our analysis.

Islamic Banks Specificities 

Regulatory requirements can be based on AAOIFI standards which 
give the local regulators the discretion to deduct a portion of the 
activity financed by PSIAs. This might result in situation where the IB 
capitalization would be much lower than similar conventional bank. 
The risk transfer from  shareholders to PSIAs’ holders is rarely 
effective (for on BS PSIAs).   

Very limited use of hybrid instruments in Islamic finance but this is 
developing. Hybrid sukuk were structured based on Mudaraba 
structure with real risk transfer (i.e. deduction from the principal in 
case of misperformance of the underlying asset (ex. NCB sukuk). 
IFSB recognizes only Musharaka sukuk as eligible for capital content. 
However, Musharaka sukuk was not used because it would likely 
result in a significant cost of funding for the bank.  

Earnings 
capacity 

Sometime can be higher for Islamic banks that are purely retail 
players (Al Rajhi Bank) but this is the exception rather than the norm.

12:15 - 14:15

   Session 2: Rulings and effects of commitment to charity in case of procrastination of 
debt settlement
  Main topics
   Drafting this expressed condition in contracts in accordance with Shari’ah: valid and 
invalid and the drafting that serves as point of reference for the courts.
   Is commitment to charity absolute or restricted to procrastination to ascertain the right 
to decide about charity payment?
   Can the creditor institution waive the implementation of the condition [of commitment 
to charity] from the customer (the debtor) who falls under the stated condition?
   What is included in the actual direct costs that the creditor institution may deduct from 
the amounts of the commitment  to charity, and the rules for calculating such amounts 
to prevent the institution from benefiting (in income) from any part of such amounts 
and the approval of the Shari’ah board for the calculation?
   Is it permitted to deduct the actual direct costs from the amounts of the commitment 
to charity without prescribing it in the contract?
   Controls for the disbursement of the amounts of the commitment to charity: avenues 
of disbursement, the approval of the Shari’ah board for disbursement and the benefit 
of the institution by publicising the disbursements.
   Can the avenues of disbursement include: disbursement to needy employees (in 
treatment, children’s education and settlement of obligations) that the institution is not 
obliged to cover by virtue of the contract or was not obligated by law to do so?
   Can the avenues of disbursement include: community activities that financial 
institutions are expected or required to support?
   Is it required to disburse the charity amount immediately? Can the institution deposit 
the charity amount in a current account held with the institution? Can the institution 
invest such amounts for the purpose of growing the funds before disbursing them?

Objective of the standard 

The objective of this standard is to establish the principles of financial reporting related to 
instruments classified as quasi-equity, such as investment accounts and similar instruments invested 
with the Islamic financial institutions (IFIs / the institutions). Quasi-equity is an element of financial 
statements of an institution in line with the “AAOIFI Conceptual Framework for Financial Reporting2” 
(the conceptual framework).

The standard shall apply to all instruments that meet the definition of quasi-equity in line with the
