From the desk of the Secretary General

Greetings from AAOIFI!

This special issue of the AAOIFI newsletter brings together the key highlights from both 
halves of 2024. In future, we will ensure its half-yearly frequency to keep our stakeholders 
updated on AAOIFI’s milestones, activities, industry collaborations, advocacy efforts and 
ongoing projects.

The year 2024 marked another pivotal chapter in AAOIFI’s journey as a global leader in 
Islamic finance standard-setting and infrastructure. It was a period of meaningful progress, 
institutional growth and strategic collaborations – driven by the collective dedication of our 
institutional members, Board of Trustees (BoT), Public Interest Monitoring Consultative Committee (PIMCC) and the 
technical boards including the AAOIFI Shari’ah Board (ASB), Accounting Board (AAB), Governance and Ethics Board 
(AGEB) and Education Board (AEB) along with their working groups, sub-committees and the Secretariate.

During 2024, AAOIFI expanded its institutional membership by 10.59%, welcoming several prominent regulatory and 
supervisory authorities. This growth further deepened industry engagement and enhanced regulatory alignment with 
AAOIFI standards. Also, a number of MoUs were signed with leading organisations across regions, reinforcing AAOIFI’s 
role as a catalyst for standard-setting and innovation in Islamic finance.

In the field of capacity-building, AAOIFI conducted a wide range of initiatives, including meetings on curriculum 
development, examinations, translations and committee activities. Additionally, 46 workshops – delivered in 
English, Arabic and Russian – were 
organised both online and in-person, 
in collaboration with AAOIFI’s global 
training partners.

  >>   Inside this issue

The year also saw the successful 
organisation of AAOIFI’s flagship event, 
the AAOIFI-IsDB 19th Annual Islamic 
Banking and Finance Conference.

In pursuit of AAOIFI’s commitment to 
hosting conferences beyond Bahrain, 
the second Islamic Capital Markets 
Conference was successfully organised 
in collaboration with the Securities and 
Exchange Commission of Pakistan (SECP).

I extend my heartfelt gratitude to all 
stakeholders for their unwavering 
support and commitment and look 
forward to sharing further updates 
on AAOIFI’s progress and initiatives, 
Insha’Allah.

AAOIFI developments

>> AAOIFI Shari’ah Board (ASB)

In 2024, the AAOIFI Shari’ah Board (ASB) held three in-person meetings and one virtual meeting to thoroughly review 
over 500 industry comments on the exposure draft (ED) of the Sukuk standard. After careful consideration, the ASB 
incorporated relevant amendments and was prepared to finalise the standard. However, recognising its significance, 
the board honoured the Secretariate’s request to extend the comment period, allowing for additional feedback from 
stakeholders.

ideas and promoting transparency in the standard-setting process.

THE NEXT GENERATION OF ISLAMIC BANKS 

Dr. Ahmed TAHIRI JOUTI,  
Chief Operating Officer, Al Maali Group 

Abstract  

Purpose ― the paper aims at defining the Business Model of the Islamic Banking Industry for the next 
decades. 
Design/methodology/approach ― the paper is based on literature review to define the main strengths, 
weaknesses, opportunities and threats facing the industry. Based on the results of the S.W.O.T analysis, 
the  paper  presents  a  vision  of  the  characteristics  of  the  transformative  Business  model  that  would 
optimize the industry position. 
Findings ― the paper emphasizes on the necessity of building sound partnerships with new entrants 
and on accelerating digitization in order to optimize the operating cost of the model and enhance its 
effectiveness.  
Research  limitations/implications  ―  the  paper  is  based  on  a  theoretical  assessment  of  the  possible 
Business Model transformations supposed to occur in the next decade. This insight could be challenged 
with Islamic Bankers to understand better the Islamic Banks ‘perspective. 
Practical  implications  ―  the  main  trends  that  would  command  the  future  of  Islamic  banking  is 
collaboration with new entrants through different digital channels. 
Originality/Value ― This paper presents the transformation of the Islamic banking model and its impact 
on different aspects such as sharia governance, partnerships and others. 
Keywords ―Islamic banking, new entrants, digitization, platforms, MSP, P2P, I2P. 
Paper type ― Conceptual paper 

Introduction 

The Prediction of any industry future is a perilous adventure. Relying on the customers’ expectations1 
or the incumbents’ plans would not pave the way for any reliable projections let alone limitless artistic 
visions2 or wishes. Nevertheless, this exercise is very important for companies to have an insight into 
where to focus their effort and money while helping them to anticipate both the moves of competitors 
and threats from outsiders3 (SCHILLING; 2017).  

An industry’s transformation is generally associated with the adoption of a new business model that 
links  a  new  technology  to  an  emerging  market  need4  (KAVADIAS  and  al.;  2016).  Nevertheless,  to 
measure the potential of a new technology5 (ADNER, 2016) and its ability to replace an old one, it is 
important to estimate the additional development required before it can be ready for commercial prime 

 1 

Henry Ford once said: If I had asked people what they wanted, they would have said a faster horse.

2 Take, for example, the series of postcards produced between 1899 and 1910 by French artist Jean-Marc Côté 
and his collaborators, who seemed confident that by the year 2000, we would have already colonized both sky 
and sea — and recruited some of their residents (whales for example) for our transit purposes. 
3 Melissa Schilling (2017), « What’s your best innovation bet? » Harvard Business Review, Issue July – August 
2017; P. 86-93   
4 Stelios KAVADIAS, Kostas LADAS and Christoph LOCH (2016), « The Transformative Business Model », Harvard 
Business Review, Issue October 2016, p. 91-98. 
5 Ron ADNER and RAHUL KAPOOR (2016), « Right Tech, Wrong time: How to make sure your ecosystem is ready 
for the newest technologies”, Harvard Business Review, Issue November 2016, P. 61-67.  

 
 
 
 
 
                                                           
time,  to  make  sure,  whether  it  will  be  price  competitive  and  to  assess  its  dependence  on  other 
innovations. If the new technology were dependent on other developments and other innovations to 
be ready for use, it would not replace the existing technology easily.  

Thus, predicting the future of an industry requires the mapping of all developing technologies in the 
industry but also in the related sectors. Moreover, the analysis should cover all the players that can 
influence  or  be  influenced  by  the  new  transformation  especially  with  the  growing  interconnection 
between the different economic sectors and industries. 

As  a  very  young  industry,  Islamic  banking  is  facing  many  challenges  that  can  limit  its  growth  and 
influence if they are not tackled correctly. In addition, these challenges vary from a region to another 
according to the context where Islamic financial institutions evolve6 (CIBAFI, GIBS 2018) and it is worth 
noting  that  one  of  the  main  challenges  facing  Islamic  finance  is  the  international  expansion  of  its 
activities and their standardization at a global scale. 

This research paper aims at identifying the main global challenges and trends facing the Islamic banking 
industry over the next decades, then, it aims at defining the new business model for Islamic banks in 
terms of distribution channels, product policy, customer relationship, HR policy, Corporate and Sharia 
Governance.  

The first part of the paper presents the different challenges facing the Islamic banking industry and the 
different trends that are influencing and that will influence the industry during the next decades. In this 
part, the survival of the concept of ‘Bank’ will be discussed from different dimensions. The second part 
will present a suggested business model based on global trends and financial technologies.  

1.  The main challenges and trends facing the Islamic banking industry 

In 2016, the global gross financial assets climbed to a new record high of 169,2 Trillion Euros7 (Allianz 
Global Wealth Report; 2017) – 198 Trillion USD- while the global Islamic financial assets  reached 1,5 
Trillion  USD8  (Islamic  Financial  Services  Industry  Stability  Report;  2017).  Thus,  the  Market  Share  of 
Islamic finance represents 0,75% in the global finance.   

Based on a High-level analysis, this weak share of Islamic finance can be justified by the limited number 
of countries adopting Islamic finance and its Market share in countries already allowing sharia compliant 
activities. Indeed, apart from Iran and Sudan that are pure Islamic financial systems, the market share 
of sharia compliant finance in the other countries does not exceed 60% in the case of Brunei and Saudi 
Arabia and 30% for countries like Bahrain, UAE, Jordan and Pakistan (Islamic Financial Services Industry 
Stability Report; 2017). Moreover, countries that are hosting Islamic financial institutions are not always 
implementing adequate infrastructure for the industry including Capital Markets. Generally, they are 
limited to banking and Takaful (Algeria, Tunisia, Egypt, Jordan, e.g.)

Therefore,  increasing  significantly  the  Market  Share  of  Islamic  finance  remains  the  major  challenge 
facing the industry at a global scale that hides minor challenges to handle at a local scale. To do so, it is 
necessary  to  complete  the  legal  and  regulatory  frameworks  to  implement  a  comprehensive  Islamic 
financial  system  reinforcing  Sharia  credibility  and  economic  efficiency.  Moreover,  Islamic  financial 
institutions need to have an adaptable business model that can evolve in different contexts.   

From another perspective, the Global Islamic Banking Survey 2018 (CIBAFI, 2018) identified the three 
top challenges for Islamic banks at a global scale based on 103 responses from 31 countries. Indeed, 
Shareholders’ value and expectations, information technology and compliance are the main concerns 
of Islamic banks. Moreover, the survey identified other challenges facing the industry at  local scales 
such  as Risk Management, Macro-economic environment and political uncertainty, Business Growth 
and expansion, Human resources and talent development, capital adequacy, corporate governance and 
Sharia compliance. 

Overall, the main challenges facing the Islamic banking industry are as follow: 

Figure 1: The main challenges facing the Islamic banking industry, Source: Author’s own 

1.1  Regulation 

1.1.1.  Regulation as a lever for development 

The involvement of regulators and their willingness to implement a competitive industry is the key factor 
of  success  of  any  experience.  If  this  involvement  were  reflected  in  a  comprehensive  and  sound 
regulation, the impact on the industry development would be very significant.  

  Why do we need a comprehensive Islamic Financial System? 

A  comprehensive  Islamic  financial  system  is  necessary  for  an  Islamic  banking  sector  to  be  price 
competitive.  For  example,  in  the  absence  of  an  Islamic  Money  Market,  Islamic  banks  would  use 
conventional money market instruments (case 1) or would be obliged to keep a significant share of their 
resources in the form of liquid assets (case 2) in order to face potential massive withdrawal of funds. If 
Sharia  compliant  capital  markets’  instruments  were  not  available,  Islamic  banks  would  invest  in 
conventional  instruments  (case  1)  or  invest  in  assets  that  do  not  generate  any  return  (case  2)  (for 
example in a Central Bank cash deposit) 

In case 1, Islamic banks would be using non-sharia compliant instruments but would keep the same level 
of return (compared to conventional banks) for both shareholders and Investment accounts’ holders. In

3 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Preface  

PR1 

Significant growth in the Takaful industry has been observed in the last couple of decades. 
However, the industry still suffers from weak infrastructure that requires organised and 
coordinated strengthening efforts. Particularly, due to the relatively smaller size of the 
Takaful sector, there is a shortage of re-Takaful institutions (and their respective capacity) 
available in the market, particularly in certain jurisdictions / regions. This has led Takaful 
institutions around the globe to resort to the use of conventional reinsurance on the basis of 
necessity, a practice that is addressed in AAOIFI Shari’ah Standard 41 “Islamic Reinsurance”. 

PR2 

This approach has been ongoing for some time, prompting AAOIFI Governance and Ethics 
Board (AGEB / the board) to feel the need for establishing clear guidelines to streamline the 
governance process and define the internal controls necessary for the purpose of ensuring 
Shari’ah compliance in the process of determination of necessity in such scenarios. 

4 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Introduction 

Overview 

IN1 

This standard outline the set of fundamental governance principles and internal controls 
that govern the situations under which a Takaful institution may utilise conventional 
reinsurance as a means of managing risk on the basis of necessity. It provides the 
governance principles and internal control requirements of ensure Shari’ah compliance in 
this respect. This standard applies to all Takaful institutions (including window Takaful 
institution) and also to re-Takaful institutions in case of retrocession. 

Rationale for issuing this standard 

IN2 

AAOIFI Governance and Ethics Board (AGEB / the board) considered the fact that there is a 
dearth of availability of re-Takaful institutions (and their respective capacity), particularly in 
certain markets and regions, and accordingly at times resorting to conventional reinsurance 
on the basis of necessity (in line with Shari’ah principles and rules, particularly AAOIFI 
Shari’ah Standard 41 “Islamic Reinsurance”) becomes a normal practice. This creates 
practical challenges and affects the industry’s reputation and enhances the risk of Shari’ah 
non-compliance. On the other hand, not having access to conventional reinsurance under 
even the “necessary” conditions increases the risk of not managing the business risk 
properly and eventual failure of Takaful institutions and industry at large. Accordingly, a 
delicate balance between the same is necessary. Therefore, the board considered the need 
for development of this standard to address such risks.

2 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Preface  

3 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Introduction 

Overview 
IN1 

This AAOIFI Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) __, prescribes the financial reporting principles and 
disclosure requirements applicable to all internal transfers of assets between investment pools 
related to (and where material, between significant categories of) owners’ equity, quasi-equity and 
off-balance-sheet assets under management of an institution. It requires adoption and consistent 
application of accounting policies for such internal transfers in line with Shari’ah principles and rules 
and describes general disclosure requirements in this respect. 

Rationale for issuing this standard 
IN2 

The AAOIFI Accounting Board (AAB / the board) initiated, in 2018, the “AAOIFI Financial Accounting 
Standards (FAS) review and revision project” encompassing all existing AAOIFI FASs. As an outcome 
of this project, as well as, during the revision process of FAS 27 “Investment Accounts”, the board 
determined that FAS 21 “Disclosure of Transfer of Asset” requires a limited revision. 

IN3 

The board also determined that certain principles of financial reporting shall be addressed in 
addition to the disclosure requirements which were included in the predecessor standard.  
Accordingly, this revised standard has been developed to address these requirements, as well as, to 
align with “AAOIFI Conceptual Framework for Financial Reporting” (the conceptual framework) and 
FAS 1 “General Presentation and Disclosures in the Financial Statements”.  

Key changes from previous standard 
IN4 

This standard (FAS ___) brings key changes from its predecessor standard (FAS 21 “Disclosure on 
Transfer of Assets”), inter alia, with respect to the following aspects: 

a. 

b. 

c. 

d. 

e. 

the earlier standard was limited to the disclosure requirements of transfer of assets. The 
revised standard prescribes the financial reporting requirements, as well as, the general and 
specific disclosure requirements;  

the revised standard requires accounting police(s) to be developed, consistently applied and 
disclosed;    

the terminology and definitions in the standard are aligned with “AAOIFI Conceptual 
Framework for Financial Reporting”; 

the presentation and disclosure requirements have been improved in line with FAS 1 
“General Presentation and Disclosures in the Financial Statements”; and 

the financial reporting requirements are aligned with the Shari’ah requirements on transfer 
of assets between investment pools by providing definitions of tangibility ratio, monetary 
assets and non-monetary assets.

AAOIFI Financial Accounting Standard __  
Transfer of Assets between Investment Pools 

Objective of the standard 

The objective of this standard is to establish the financial reporting principles that apply in respect of 
internal transfer of assets between various investment pools of an Islamic financial institution (the 
institution).  

Scope 

This standard shall apply to all transfers of assets between investment pools related to (and where 
material, between significant categories of) owners’ equity, quasi-equity and off-balance-sheet 
assets under management of an institution.  

The assets transferred, for the purpose of application of this standard, include all monetary and non-
monetary assets. 

Definitions 

For the purpose of interpreting and applying this standard, the following short definitions are 
relevant: 

a. 

b. 

c. 

Investment pool – in the specific context of this standard, comprises of distinct assets whose 
risks and rewards (wholly or proportionately) are attributable to the funding provided by 
respective investors within owners’ equity, quasi-equity and off-balance-sheet assets under 
management of an institution. It is generally a virtual entity and at times a separate 
reporting entity; 

Monetary assets – in the specific context of this standard, for the purpose of computation of 
the tangibility ratio, are assets that are in the form of either: 

i. 

ii. 

cash;  

contractual right to receive cash or another monetary asset from another institution 
or entity; or 

iii. 

contractual right to exchange a monetary asset with another institution or entity. 

Non-monetary assets – in the specific context of this standard, for the purpose of 
computation of the tangibility ratio, in line with the Shari’ah principles and rules, may 
include: 

i. 

tangible assets (i.e., assets that are physical such as property, plant, equipment and 
inventory); 

ii. 

equity instruments; 

5 

 
 
 
 
 
iii. 

Sukuk or other instruments whereby the majority of underlying assets are tangible 
assets; and 

iv. 

intangible assets (e.g., right of use assets). 

d. 

e. 

f. 

Shari’ah principles and rules – is as defined in paragraph 11 of the “AAOIFI Conceptual 
Framework for Financial Reporting1”; 

Tangibility ratio – in the specific context of this standard, is the ratio defined by an 
institution in line with Shari’ah principles and rules, acceptable for the purpose of transfer of 
an asset or a bundle of assets from investment pool to another when it involves debt; and 

Transfer of assets – in the specific context of this standard, is the transfer of assets from one 
investment pool to another (whether on-balance-sheet or off-balance-sheet) by way of 
either sale, exchange, investment, clearing or others (e.g., Hiba) in line with a contractual 
arrangement, complying with Shari’ah principles and rules.

• TAC will be required to fundamentally change the way it 

operates and does business today. This will necessitate a 

redesign of its business model.  

• TAC’s primary competitive advantage will be its exceptional 

customer focus. For the successful execution of its 5 year 

transformation and growth strategy, TAC will: 

• Strive to become a lean and efficient organization… driven by 

a new operating model that institutionalizes company wide 

accountability 

• Transform the collections framework to align with leading 

market practices 

• Build a technology oriented, sales-focused organization that is 

energized by a strong performance-driven culture 

• TAC’s strategy 2014 – 2018 can be summarized comprising of 

3 core pillars, 15 key initiatives and 33 major projects. 

• Through the implementation of this strategy, TAC aims at 

emerging as a leading market player, with a strong corporate 

identity, fully compliant with regulations and an employer of 

choice for competent human resource.  

• TAC also aims to achieve a sustainable Return on Asset 

(ROA) of [3.1 – 3.5]% by 2018.

Targets final consumption

VAT targets consumption, not the decisions leading up to consumption or the production of

business value chain and may change 
decisions 

Broad base 

…apart from the exceptions 

Good VAT systems attempt to tax as much as possible and in as standardized a fashion as 
possible. It is applied to goods and services. Generally it looks not at the supplier, but what is 
being supplied and should not create a wedge between similar product types

needs. Exemptions and zero-rates are 
problematic 

Burden on consumers, not business 

…most of the time 

Welfare loss (through actual cost and a revision to cost-based spending decisions) under VAT is 
intended to be shouldered by consumers, not businesses. To some extent this is not necessarily 
true in all sectors 

The challenge is that exemption forces 
the burden onto the intermediary, or 
builds in the burden into the supply chain

AAOIFI Financial Accounting Standard __  
Off-balance-sheet Assets Under Management 

Objective of the standard 

The objective of this standard is to establish the principles of financial reporting related to off-
balance-sheet assets under management in line with the “AAOIFI Conceptual Framework for 
Financial Reporting1” (the conceptual framework).  

Scope 

This standard shall be applicable on all Islamic financial institutions (the institutions) preparing 
financial statements in line with the requirements of AAOIFI Financial Accounting Standards (FASs), 
with regard to the assets managed in a fiduciary capacity without establishing control, except for: 

the participants’ Takaful fund and / or participants’ investment fund of a Takaful institution2; 
and 

an investment fund managed by the institution, being a separate legal entity, which is 
subject to financial reporting in line with the requirement of the relevant AAOIFI FAS3.

Difference in conventional and Islamic 
accounting 

Islamic accounting 

Conventional accounting 

•

Islamic accounting is defined by the 
AAOIFI as: 

• “Accounting process which 

provides appropriate information 
not necessarily limited to financial 
data to stakeholders of an entity 
which will enable them to ensure 
that entity is continuously 
operating within the boundaries of 
Shari'ah and delivering on its 
socioeconomic objectives.” 

• Accounting is defined by the 

AICPA as: 

• “the art of recording, classifying, 
and summarizing in a significant 
manner and in terms of money, 
transactions and events which 
are, in part at least, of financial 
character, and interpreting the 
results thereof.“

Objectives and focus 

Conventional accounting 
• Conventional accounting aims to 
permit informed decisions by 
information users, whose 
ultimate purpose is, to efficiently 
allocate scarce resources 
available to the best efficient and 
profitable manner. 

• Conventional accounting 

concentrates on the information 
identifying economic activities 
and transactions. 

Islamic accounting 

• Islamic accounting abides by the 
principles of Shari'ah and rules in 
their dealings and to assess 
whether the objectives of the 
organization are being met.  
• Islamic accounting also identifies 
and deals with certain socio-
economic factors, religious 
activities and transactions.

Time value of money 

Fair value vs. historical cost

Concept of quasi-equity.  

• There is a view that for the purpose of 
better accounting, comparability and 
transparency, the concept of time value of 
money should be applied in accounting for 
Islamic finance. 

• On the other hand, if time value of money 
concept is accepted in accounting for 
Islamic finance then Islamic banking  may 
no longer be termed as Islamic in its 
essence. 
In an interest free economic system, the 
discount rate should always be zero and 
hence the present value of receivables 
should be equivalent to their par value.

3. Perceived gaps in FAS
What are the perceived gaps in FAS compared to other global acceptable 
accounting practices? 

One of the major challenges facing IFIs lies in the preparation of the financial
statements under different accounting standards and which may lead to
problems of comparability, reliability and compliance level measurement. This
has resulted in a heated debate among scholars which has hitherto translated
to the evolving existing literature surrounding the interpretation of the level of
compliance with the Islamic accounting standards.

Development

IFRS are developed and continuously being developed.
•
• Unfortunate fact that there is no significant effort on
standardization of accounting principles in Islam.
AAOIFI standards cater to the IFIs only, and not all
the businesses in general.

Information Needs

Information needs of Muslim or Shari'ah compliant stakeholders differ 
significantly from a conventional stakeholder. This is due to its 
information needs for computation of Zakat, profit and loss distribution, 
screening of permissible investments and laws of inheritance.

Accounting Principles 

Differences in accounting principles arising because of
rejection of conventional
financial system and its
transactions by the Islamic economic system or rather
the Islamic financial system, to be specific.

Part A: Introduction and users’ guide  

Objective 

1. 

2. 

The Islamic financial institutions (IFIs) are required to publish periodic financial statements to satisfy 
the common information needs of the users1. Accounting and Auditing Organization for Islamic 
Financial Institutions (AAOIFI) has issued Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) 1 “General Presentation 
and Disclosures in the Financial Statements”, to be read in conjunction with the conceptual 
framework, which sets out the overall requirements for presentation of the financial statements, the 
minimum requirements for the contents of the financial statements and a recommended structure of 
financial statements that facilitate faithful presentation in line with Shari’ah principles and rules and 
comparability with the IFIs’ financial statements of previous periods, and the financial statements of 
other institutions. 

This accounting technical release (ATR) aims to guide the Islamic banks and similar financial institutions 
(the Islamic banks) applying FAS 1 “General Presentation and Disclosures in the Financial Statements” 
along with other relevant AAOIFI FASs, with regard to their practical application for the purpose of 
preparation of their financial statements, duly complying with the Shari’ah principles and rules and 
aligned with the generally accepted accounting principles (see paragraph 7 of the conceptual 
framework).  

Scope  

3. 

4. 

5. 

These illustrative financial statements provide guidance to the Islamic banks, in line with the 
requirements of Parts 1 and 2 of FAS 1 “General Presentation and Disclosures in the Financial 
Statements”, along with other relevant AAOIFI FASs, for understanding and implementing the 
standards with regard to preparation and presentation of their financial statements.  

These illustrative financial statements address presentation and disclosure requirements for the 
consolidated financial statements, as well as, the stand-alone or separate financial statements of the 
Islamic banks. 

The term “the Islamic banks”, for the purpose of this ATR, shall primarily include Islamic retail banks 
and Islamic commercial banks and insofar as it relates, may include:

Rationale of development and issuance of this ATR 

8. 

9. 

It was observed by the AAOIFI Accounting Board (AAB) that the disclosure requirements contained in 
FAS 1 “General Presentation and Disclosures in the Financial Statements”, as well as, other AAOIFI 
FASs, along with additional disclosure requirements of generally accepted accounting principles, 
required further explanation and demonstration from a practical guidance perspective.  

AAB decided that the illustrative financial statements, which were earlier part of FAS 1 “General 
Presentation and Disclosures in the Financial Statements” (issued in 1996, now superseded), shall be 
issued by the AAOIFI Technical Advisory and Interpretation Committee (TAIC) as a separate document, 
with lower authoritative status, and would be subject to periodic updates due to the ever-changing 
nature of the financial services industry, and in line with advancements in Islamic banking practices. 
This shall facilitate addressing new developments which may arise from time to time.  

Authoritative status and relationship with AAOIFI FASs and other standards 

10. 

11. 

This ATR shall be read in conjunction with FAS 1 “General Presentation and Disclosures in the Financial 
Statements” and other relevant AAOIFI FASs. Where applicable, it shall also be read in conjunction with 
other generally accepted accounting principles (insofar as these are not inconsistent with the 
conceptual framework and Shari’ah principles and rules as required by paragraph 1653 of FAS 1 
“General Presentation and Disclosures in the Financial Statements”) for the areas which are not 
covered by AAOIFI FASs.  

In the event of an inconsistency between the requirements of this ATR and the provisions of any 
standard (including AAOIFI FASs or other generally accepted accounting principles (see paragraphs 8 
and 10)), which may arise due to a specific situation or revision of such standard, the requirements of 
the respective standard shall prevail.

AAOIFI Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) __ “Financial Reporting for Islamic Financial Services Offered by 
Conventional Financial Institutions” is set out in paragraphs 01-14. All the paragraphs have equal authority. 
This standard should be read in the context of its objective and the Conceptual Framework for Financial 
Reporting as endorsed by AAOIFI. 

All AAOIFI FASs shall be read in conjunction with the definitions, Shari’ah principles and rules and key 
considerations provided by AAOIFI Shari’ah Standards (SS) in respect of such products and matters.   

3 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Preface 

Islamic financial institutions are not the only providers of Islamic financial services. Shari’ah 
compliant products and services are also offered by other conventional financial institutions, in an 
increasing number. At times, the Shari’ah compliant products and services constitute an insignificant 
part of the conventional institution’s business. Whereas, for other conventional financial 
institutions, the Shari’ah compliant products and business has become a material part. As the 
demand for Islamic financial services grow, it is expected that more conventional institutions shall 
start offering Shari’ah compliant products and services.  

Due to the expected demand from the industry, as identified by the different stakeholders at various 
forums, the AAOIFI Accounting Board (AAB / board) initiated the revision of FAS 18 “Islamic Financial 
Services Offered by Conventional Financial Institutions”. It is expected that the revised standard shall 
improve the overall accounting and financial reporting practices by giving clarity to conventional 
financial institutions offering Islamic finance services, to present their Shari’ah compliant operations 
in a transparent manner. 

4 

 
 
 
 
 
Introduction 

Overview 
IN1 

This Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) __ “Financial Reporting for Islamic Financial Services 
Offered by Conventional Financial Institutions” includes financial reporting requirements to 
conventional financial institutions offering Islamic financial services.  

IN2 

This standard requires that conventional financial institutions offering Islamic financial services 
through an Islamic finance window shall prepare and present the financial statements of the Islamic 
finance window in line with the requirements of this standard, read with other AAOIFI FASs. It 
prescribes the presentation and disclosure requirements applicable on the Islamic finance windows, 
as well as, the basic financial reporting requirements. 

IN3 

This standard supersedes the earlier FAS 18 “Islamic Financial Services Offered by Conventional 
Financial Institutions”.  

Rationale for issuing this standard  
IN4 

The AAOIFI Accounting Board (AAB / the board), in 2018, initiated the “AAOIFI Financial Accounting 
Standards (FAS) review and revision project” to review and revise all existing FASs. As a result of the 
finalisation and issuance of the “AAOIFI Conceptual Framework for Financial Reporting (Revised 
2020)” and the revision of [ED of] FAS 1 (Revised 2021) “General Presentation and Disclosures in the 
Financial Statements”1, it was inevitable to ensure that all other FASs are aligned with the same.  

IN5 

IN6 

IN7 

The board was concerned with the increasing systemic significance of conventional financial 
institutions offering Islamic financial services over the past decades, particularly in certain significant 
Islamic finance markets. The board took a note of the fact that this trend is expected to continue in 
the foreseeable future.  

It is also important that the Islamic financial services offered by such conventional financial 
institutions are perceived to comply with established Shari’ah principles and rules, while significant 
improvement is needed, at times, with regards to their compliance with the Shari’ah principles and 
rules, governance requirements, as well as, financial reporting requirements. This makes it all the 
more important to improve the financial reporting requirements to ensure adequate level of 
compliance, transparency and disclosure practices. 

Further taking into consideration the significance and growth of Islamic financial windows, as well

● Price = Benchmark + spread

depending on supply and demand conditions
● Actual price might be higher or lower than the 

Benchmark

● The Benchmark serves as a guiding reference for 

banks and investors

● Example: negatives interest rate

● Interest-indicators should converge to economic 

fundamentals reflected by the Benchmark

● When the information is widely used, investors will 

align pricing with the Benchmark

From the desk

Greetings from AAOIFI! 

It is my pleasure to present the first issue of the AAOIFI Newsletter in 2025, covering our key 
achievements, milestones and initiatives during the first half of the year. This periodical serves 
as a medium to keep our valued stakeholders informed about AAOIFI’s progress in standard-
setting, institutional development, global collaborations and advocacy efforts dedicated to 
advancing Islamic finance worldwide. 

The first six months of 2025 have been marked by significant accomplishments across AAOIFI’s 
technical boards – the Shari’ah Board (ASB), Accounting Board (AAB), Governance and Ethics 
Board (AGEB) and Education Board (AEB) – demonstrating our continued leadership in 
developing, refining and harmonising international standards for Islamic finance. The 
issuance, translation and review of standards during this period highlight our steadfast 
commitment to quality, Shari’ah authenticity and industry relevance. 

Mr. Omar Mustafa Ansari 
Secretary General, AAOIFI 

Equally noteworthy was the successful organisation of two international events, furthering our mission to foster dialogue and collaboration. The 
1st International Conference on Partnership Finance and Investments was organised in Kazan, the capital of the Republic of 
Tatarstan—an autonomous Muslim majority region of  the Russian Federation. This landmark event marked was jointly organised 
by AAOIFI, the Tatarstan Investment Development Agency (TIDA) and the Ministry of Economy of the Republic of Tatarstan, with Sberbank 
PJSC taking role of the strategic partner. The conference was a part of the 16th International Economic Forum themed “Russia-Islamic 
World: KazanForum 2025” and marked a new chapter  in AAOIFI’s global engagement, strengthening partnerships with 
regulators, academics and financial institutions in emerging markets. 

The 23rd Annual Shari’ah Boards Conference, held under the esteemed patronage of the Central Bank of Bahrain, brought 
together leading scholars, regulators, policymakers and industry experts from more than 20 countries to deliberate on 
contemporary Shari’ah and legal issues shaping the future of Islamic finance. During this period, AAOIFI also deepened its 
strategic relationships through new Memoranda of Understanding (MoUs) with international organisations, expanded its 
institutional membership, and continued to enhance professional capacity through multilingual training programmes and 
certification initiatives across regions.  

As we reflect on these achievements, I extend my sincere appreciation to our Board of Trustees (BoT), Public Interest Monitoring 
Consultative Committee (PIMCC), technical boards, institutional members, and global partners for their unwavering dedication 
and support. Their collective contributions remain the cornerstone of AAOIFI’s growth and impact. We move into the second half 
of the year with renewed energy and commitment to promoting integrity, standardisation and Shari’ah compliance in the global 
Islamic finance industry – Insha’Allah.

Standards development 

AAOIFI Shari’ah Board (ASB) 

During the first half of 2025, the AAOIFI Shari’ah Board (ASB) focused on refining and finalising key Shari’ah standards (SS), 
particularly the exposure draft of SS 62 “Sukuk”. The Shari’ah Board conducted public hearings and consultation process to 
gather industry feedback and review issues regarding Sukuk structures, guarantees and tradability according to the Shari’ah 
principles. In the meetings, jurisprudential matters related to the combination of contracts, promises and reinsurance in 
Takaful were also discussed. Through these sessions, the Board ensured that the emerging standards were comprehensive, 
practical and aligned with Shari’ah objectives. Two board meeting were held in the first half of 2025 to review multiple 
Shari’ah standards and a press release was issued regarding the draft Shari’ah Standard No. 62 “Sukuk” on 5 May 2025. 
The ASB continued to oversee the implementation of the existing standards as well.

AAOIFI Accounting Board (AAB) 

The AAOIFI Accounting Board (AAB) took several significant steps to enhance accounting standards for Islamic financial 
institutions. It finalised and issued the Arabic versions of FAS 42 “Presentation and Disclosures in the Financial Statements of 
Takaful Institutions” and FAS 44 “Determining Control of Assets and Business”, aligning them with their previously released 
English editions. 

It also reviewed the draft of the proposed standard on the same topic, approved in principle the new FAS 51 “Participatory 
Ventures” and held a public hearing on the revised FAS 25 “Investments in Sukuk, Shares and Similar Instruments”. These 
actions reflected AAB’s sustained commitment to improving transparency, harmonisation and Shari’ah alignment in Islamic 
accounting practices.

A comprehensive sharia compliant financial system 
Learnings 

A  comprehensive  Islamic  financial  system  can 
ensure  more  economic  efficiency  &  sharia 
credibility to the Islamic financial products. 

The  Islamic 
financial  system  needs  to  be 
extended  to  all  the  components  dealing  with 
Islamic  financial  institutions  and  not  limited  to 
Islamic financial institutions. 

If  there  is  a  single  entity  in  charge  of 
regulation of the whole financial system, it’s 
very  important  to  draft  a  roadmap  and  to 
share it with the different stakeholders.  
The  roadmap  needs  to  be  detailed  and 
defines  realistic  deadlines.  It  makes  the 
whole approach trustworthy 

If  there  are  different  entities  in  charge  of 
regulation  of  the  financial  system.  It’s 
important  to  create  a  committee  in  charge 
of the coordination. 
This  committee  has  to  draft  a  detailed 
roadmap with realistic deadlines. 

This  approach  involves  many  stakeholders  and  players.  Thus, 
trustworthiness  and  respect  of  deadlines  is  a  real  key  success  factor 
for the experience.

JOIFA - Call for papers – 9th issue 

Dear scholars, researchers and Islamic finance professionals, 

Accounting and Auditing Organisation for Islamic Financial Institutions (AAOIFI) is excited to announce the call 
for papers for the next issue of JOIFA which is dedicated to advancing knowledge and promoting research in 
the field of Islamic finance including, accountancy, governance, Shari’ah advisory, auditing and ethics, and 
themes under AAOIFI’s mandate. We invite scholars, researchers, and practitioners from around the world to 
submit their original research papers, technical articles, book reviews, case studies etc. to be considered for 
publication in JOIFA.  

Please read the guidelines for authors before submitting your manuscript, to ensure it is consistent with the 
journal style and format (https://aaoifi.com/guidelines-for-authors/?lang=en). Papers will undergo at least one 
double blind-peer review in addition to review by the editorial desk. Final acceptance of approved papers will 
be contingent on incorporating reviewers’ feedback to the satisfaction of the reviewers and/or editorial team. 
You may download previous issues of JOIFA from this link: https://aaoifi.com/joifa/download-joifa/?lang=en. 
For any queries, please contact the editor via the email: editor.joifa@aaoifi.com. 

Potential contributions may include, but might not be limited to, theoretical, empirical and technical papers 
that consider the following topics / areas. 

Accounting related topics  

1.  Control (of assets and business) 
2.  Determination of Direct Expenses 
3.  Financial reporting for institutions operating in hyperinflationary economies   
4.  Gifts and prizes 
5.  Off-balance-sheet assets under management 
6.  Quasi-equity (including investment accounts) 
7.  Transfer of assets between investment pools 
8.  Zakah computational guidelines for Islamic finance institutions 

Papers and technical articles are also invited on below given topics for AAOIFI Financial Accounting 
Standards (FAS) under revision which includes the following FASs:  

9.  FAS 3 “Mudaraba Financing and FAS 4 “Musharaka Financing” 
10. FAS 7 “Salam and Parallel Salam” and FAS 10 “Istisna’a and Parallel Istisna’a” 
11. FAS 14 “Investment Funds”  
12. FAS 23 “Consolidation” 
13. FAS 24 “Investments in Associates”  
14. FAS 26 “Investment in Real estate”

Guidelines for authors 

1.  The author(s) should not mention their names and affiliation, etc. on the text of the paper. A separate 

sheet bearing full name, affiliation, mailing address and telephone number should be sent along with the 
main paper.  

2.  The papers submitted for publication must not have been submitted simultaneously to any other journal. 
3.  All papers must have an abstract of no more than 300 words, 4-6 Keywords and JEL and KAUJIE 

classification, if applicable. The ‘Abstract’ should contain the objective, methodology, findings and 
suggestions, and practical implications of the research work for publication. 
4.  The length of the article should preferably be between 4000- 8,000 words. 
5.  The papers should make some significant contribution to the above topics either theoretical or applied, or 

discuss any related professional aspect of Islamic finance. 

6.  Submitted papers must contain original unpublished work. The subject content should not impinge upon, 

violate or constitute a breach of any intellectual or business rights of any third party. 

7.  All papers should have an introductory section in which the objectives and methodology of the article are 

explained and a final/ concluding section, which summarises the main points discussed, and the 
conclusions reached.  

8.  The AAOIFI shall have non-exclusive rights on the materials published in the JOIFA; would be fully 

authorised to use or distribute the material for the purpose of Indexing / Abstracting, to translate into 
other languages, or for any other related purposes; and would have power to enter into any agreement 
with third parties for distribution of the materials by any means and in any format. 

9.  Since all material received for publication is to be peer reviewed, two hard copies or a soft editable copy 

should be submitted in final form for sending anonymously to the reviewers.  

10. The authors would have to revise and resend the papers in the light of comments / suggestions by the peer 

reviewers or the editorial team. 

11. References in APA style should be listed at the end of the text in the following style:  

Articles: El-Gari, Mohamed Ali (2003); Credit Risk in Islamic Banking and Finance; Islamic Economic Studies; 
Vol. 10, No. 2, (2003); Pp. 1 – 23;  
Books: Khan, A. R. (1993), Financial Intermediation, New York: Springer Publishers. 
Standards / Reports: Issuing institution, Number, Title, year of publication, with clauses / sub clauses of the 
relevant standards 
Page references to works referred to in the text should take the following form: El-Gamal (2006:128). 
The verses of the Holy Qur’ān quoted should carry Surah number and ãyah number as (2:275). 
Complete reference to the source of Hādīth quoted should be given like Muslim, Kitab al Shirkah

WHAT ARE CRYPTOCURRENCIES? 

Definition: “virtual currency” is a digital representation of value 
that :

can be digitally traded and functions as a medium of 
exchange, and/or a unit of account; and/or a store of value, 

does not have legal tender status in any jurisdiction and not 
issued nor guaranteed by any jurisdiction,

fulfills the above functions only by agreement within the 
community of users of the virtual currency. 

(FATF Report – Virtual Currencies)

Smart contracts help you 
exchange money, property, 
shares, or anything of value in 
a transparent, conflict-free 
way while avoiding the 
services of a middleman.

• Pre-written logic in the form of computer code
• Stored and replicated on the blockchain
• Executed and run by the network of computers running the blockchain
• Can result in updates to accounts on the ledger (i.e. payment for an 

executed contract)

Smart contracts can be 
programmed using basic logic. 
On the most basic level, they 
can:

• Perform calculations (i.e. calculating profit)
• Store information (i.e. membership records)
• Send transactions to other accounts (i.e. payment for a good or service)
• Perform Transactions

WHAT ARE THE TYPES OF 
CRYPTOCURRENCY?

1. Currency Coins – only serve as a currency in the 
peer to peer network to transfer value between
participants with no other function (Bitcoin, Litecoin, 
Ripple)

2. Tokens – Not currency coins. They are typically 
associated with a smart contract 

Sharia research has revolved around currency coins

Limited Shariah research on this although general direction is that 
they will be treated based on the underlying concept of the token

requirement to obey the ruler in permitted things

WILL ISLAMIC FINANCE INFLUENCE THE DEVELOPMENT 
OF CRYPTOCURRENCIES? IS IT RELEVANT? 

Historically, Islamic economists were calling for a PLS based system but this failed and the 
existing system is mainly debt based – crypto supporters are also looking for an alternative 

The main reason for this is the conventional fractional reserve system

• Customer Loan to bank = deposit 
• Government guarantees loans to banks
• Government allows tax deductibility for interest thus encouraging borrowing from banks
• Therefore, banks are able to borrow from customers at very low rates 
• Governments mandate paying taxes in fiat currency
• Why such special treatment for banks? Because banks allow governments to borrow 
when they are in need as well as allow the government to control the money supply 
through its control of banks and interest rates

A crypto “bank” would likely function more like a classical Islamic model with 100% reserve 
custodial services and 0% reserve investment services.

CONSIDERATIONS

Crypto lack three essential elements that stable monetary 
regimes provide:

• Protection against risk of structural deflation
• Ability to respond to temporary shocks and smoothen 

the business cycles

• Capacity to act as lender of last resort

Threat: Prevalence of Crypto can make monetary policy 
less effective

How to respond:

• Make fiat currencies better and more stable units of 

account

• Regulate the use of crypto to prevent regulatory 
arbitrage and unfair advantage from lighter 
regulation (esp. AML/TF)

• Continue to make central bank money attractive –
possibly by issuing central bank digital tokens

AAOIFI Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) __ “Promotional Gifts and Prizes” is set out in paragraphs 01-14. 
All the paragraphs have equal authority. This standard should be read in the context of its objective and the 
“AAOIFI Conceptual Framework for Financial Reporting”.  

All AAOIFI FASs shall be read in conjunction with the definitions, Shari’ah principles and rules and key 
considerations provided by AAOIFI Shari’ah standards (SSs). This standard shall be read particularly in 
conjunction with SS 55 “Competitions and Prizes”. 

3 

 
 
 
 
 
 
Preface 
PR1 

In the recent years, certain Islamic financial institutions (the institutions) have initiated offering 
certain additional benefits, such as promotional gifts or prizes, to customers who hold investment 
accounts (or at times other types of deposits) with the institution. This practice is particularly 
common in specific regions, where the routine returns on investment accounts were observed on a 
lower side, and where the conventional financial institutions have also been offering similar 
incentives for maintaining deposits with them. In addition, certain Sukuk programs have been 
introduced involving a similar structure. 

PR2 

As such promotional gifts and prizes awarded by the institutions are on a voluntary basis, and have 
certain significant Shari’ah compliance risks, AAOIFI had earlier issued a Shari’ah standard on this 
subject1. This standard aims to address the challenges on accounting and financial reporting side of 
such promotional gifts and prizes. 

1 AAOIFI SS 55 “Competitions and Prizes” 

4 

 
 
 
 
 
Introduction 

Overview 
IN1 

This standard prescribes accounting and financial reporting principles for recognition, measurement, 
presentation and disclosures applicable to promotional gifts and prizes awarded by the Islamic 
financial institutions (IFIs / the institutions) to their customers, including quasi-equity and other 
investment accountholders. 

Rationale for issuing this standard 
IN2 

During the revision process of AAOIFI Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) 27 “Investment 
Accounts”, it became evident that certain institutions have, as a common practice, initiated offering 
certain additional benefits, such as promotional gifts or prizes, to the investment accountholders 
(and at times to other stakeholders). The board noted that there is a lack of guidance on their 
accounting and financial reporting aspects, and accordingly, decided to develop a specific FAS on this 
subject. 

5 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
[Exposure Draft F4/2023 v1.1 of the]  
AAOIFI Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) ___  
Promotional Gifts and Prizes 

Objective of the standard 
1. 

The objective of this standard is to prescribe the accounting and financial reporting requirements 
applicable to promotional gifts and prizes awarded by the Islamic financial institutions (the 
institutions) to their customers, including quasi-equity and other investment accountholders. 

Scope 
2. 

This standard shall apply to promotional gifts and prizes (including loyalty programs) announced and 
awarded by an institution (on behalf of the owners, and out of the amounts attributable to the 
owners’ equity) to all or specific customers including quasi-equity and other investment 
accountholders. 

3. 

This standard shall not apply to Hiba provided by the institution on behalf of the owners as a profit 
smoothening technique, during the computation of periodic profits and losses of an investment pool 
at the time of constructive liquidation, that falls within the scope of respective AAOIFI FAS2. 

Definition 
4. 

For the purpose of interpreting and applying this standard, the following short definitions are 
relevant: 

a. 

b. 

c. 

Promotional prize – in the specific context of this standard, is a prize used to promote 
specific products or services or to attract investors by way of holding a draw or by achieving 
a defined target, or a combination of both which is awarded at a given point of time in 
future;  

Promotional gift – in the specific context of this standard, is a gift used to promote specific 
products or services or to attract investors that is awarded by an institution from time to 
time to certain customers; 

Loyalty program – is usually in the form of points accumulated by the customers against the 
purchase or continued utilisation of products or services, or for maintaining investment 
accounts, enabling the customers to exchange accumulated points for cash, goods or 
services on a complementary basis or at discounted rates at a future date.

AAOIFI Accounting Board (AAB) Statement 1/2025  
Withdrawal of FAS 26 “Investment in Real Estate” and Related 
Transitional Provisions  

Objective of the statement 

The objective of this statement is to: 

a. 

b. 

withdraw AAOIFI Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) 26 “Investment in Real Estate”; and 

provide necessary guidance (including transitional provisions) to the Islamic financial 
institutions or other institutions applying AAOIFI FASs (the institutions) in respect of 
accounting and financial reporting for investment property, as applicable in addition to the 
generally accepted accounting principles, once FAS 26 “Investment in Real Estate” stands 
withdrawn. 

Statement of withdrawal 

AAOIFI Accounting Board (AAB) has decided to withdraw FAS 26 “Investment in Real Estate” (see 
paragraph 13), considering that: 

a. 

b. 

after revision of “AAOIFI Conceptual Framework for Financial Reporting” and FAS 1 “General 
Presentation and Disclosures in the Financial Statements” there is a need for aligning the 
accounting and financial reporting requirements applicable in respect of investment 
property; and 

certain specific requirements related to the Shari’ah principles and rules and the business 
model of Islamic financial institutions contained in FAS 26 “Investment in Real Estate” are 
already provided in other relevant AAOIFI FASs1. 

Accounting and financial reporting guidance in respect of investment property 

Following the withdrawal of FAS 26 “Investment in Real Estate”, an institution shall account for 
investment property and present the same in its financial statements in line with the requirements 
of generally accepted accounting principles2, insofar as the same do not contradict with the 
requirements of any other AAOIFI FAS(s), “AAOIFI Conceptual Framework for Financial Reporting” 
and Shari’ah principles and rules3.  

1 See paragraph 148 on Statement of income and attribution related to quasi-equity of FAS 1 “General Presentation and 
Disclosures in the Financial Statements”; paragraphs 16 and 25(b) of FAS 45 “Quasi-Equity (Including Investment 
Accounts)” and paragraphs 14-15 on Reserves of FAS 46 “Off-Balance-Sheet Assets Under Management”. 
2 See hierarchy for selection and application of accounting policies provided in paragraph 165 of FAS 1 “General 
Presentation and Disclosures in the Financial Statements”. 
3 See paragraph 11(p) of the “AAOIFI Conceptual Framework for Financial Reporting” for definition of Shari’ah principles 
and rules.

Change in accounting policy 

An institution shall assess at the time of withdrawal of FAS 26 “Investment in Real Estate”, as to 
whether such withdrawal would result in a change in accounting policy for the institution, 
particularly considering the suitable business model applicable to the institution and / or applicable 
in respect of its contractual arrangements with participatory stakeholders.4 

A change in accounting policy shall be accounted for in line with the requirements of respective 
AAOIFI FAS5, subject to the specific requirements and transitional provisions prescribed in this 
statement. (See paragraphs 7-11) 

Attribution of gains, losses and reserves 

In case investment property (or an item thereof) is financed solely or jointly by participatory 
stakeholders other than common shareholders of an institution, the respective provisions of 
relevant AAOIFI FASs6 shall continue to apply, notwithstanding the withdrawal of FAS 26 
“Investment in Real Estate”. Such provisions require an institution to ensure that the relevant 
income, profits, gains, losses and reserves (including accumulated losses / negative reserves) are 
attributed between the relevant stakeholders in line with the requirements of Shari’ah principles 
and rules and contractual arrangements.   

Transitional provisions 

There is a rebuttable presumption that in most cases a constructive liquidation(s) has already taken 
place in respect of attributions to different participatory stakeholders other than common 
shareholders, hence, a full restatement application of the new accounting policy may not be 
appropriate in all cases.  

Irrespective of whether the new accounting policy is applied using a full restatement approach or a 
modified restatement approach, the balances attributable to the respective participatory 
stakeholders at the end of the last financial reporting period of application of FAS 26 “Investment in 
Real Estate” shall remain unchanged (including, unrealised gains, losses and reserves (or negative 
reserves).  

Accordingly, any “property fair value reserve” previously maintained under the provisions of FAS 26 
“Investment in Real Estate” shall be reclassified or another effect of the change as of the date of 
transition (e.g., due to change from cost model to fair value model or vice versa) be applied

b.

Introduction 

Overview 
IN1 

This AAOIFI Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) 29 “Sukuk in the Books of the Originator” 
prescribes accounting and classification of Sukuk issuance, in the books of the institution, its 
presentation and related disclosures requirements in the financial statements of the 
institution. It also provides guidance on off-balance-sheet Sukuk issued by the institution. 

Rationale for issuing this standard 
IN2 

This standard constitutes a part of a comprehensive project on Sukuk including Shari’ah, 
financial accounting and governance standards. Absence of a standard for the accounting 
and financial reporting triggered the need for this standard on a priority basis.  

IN3  While accounting for Sukuk issuance shall be significantly dependent on the accounting for 
the respective product, AAOIFI has already issued FASs for most of the common products; 
however, its presentation and classification on the liability side shall be based on the 
structure and characteristics of Sukuk. This standard aims to provide such guidance in 
particular.  

IN4 

This standard aims to provide guidance for accounting classification and presentation for 
Sukuk issuances primarily based on the Sukuk structure, which may include on-balance-
sheet, as well as, off-balance-sheet accounting. These classifications depend on the control 
of such assets comprising of power to control and nature of control i.e., for risks and 
rewards as well as varying benefits to the institution or the fiduciary responsibility on behalf 
of the Sukuk-holders.  

IN5 

This standard further provides guidance on the on-balance-sheet structures, whereby these 
may include instruments to be reported as equity, quasi-equity, financial liability or a non-
financial liability.

Preface  
PR1 

Zakah is an important religious duty and one of the pillars of Islamic economic system. AAOIFI had 
issued Financial Accounting Standard (FAS) 9 “Zakah” to help streamline the application of different 
methods used by Islamic financial institutions (the institutions) in determining the Zakah base, the 
items included in determining this base, the measurement of these items and the different degrees 
of disclosure of such bases from one Islamic institution to another. The accounting treatment of 
Zakah affects the determination of the institution net income, which is an important indicator used 
by users of the financial statements in making their decisions. The importance of standardization of 
such practices cannot be undermined.  

PR2 

PR3 

PR4 

The comprehensive revision of AAOIFI FAS 9 “Zakah” commenced in July 2016. The initial part of the 
project included the overall identification of issues in the existing standard and the need to provide 
specific guidance. Initial discussions identified major areas of accounting for Zakah that are not 
addressed in the existing standard and the revision was agreed to include a broader scope. This was 
also done keeping in view the additional accounting and computations related matters mentioned in 
the AAOIFI Shari'ah standard (SS) 35 “Zakah”.  

It is expected that the new standard will improve the overall accounting and financial reporting 
practices of the Islamic finance industry.  

This standard is to be read together with AAOIFI SS 35 “Zakah” and supersedes the earlier AAOIFI 
FAS 9 “Zakah”.

“Zakah” issued previously. This standard aims at setting out the accounting treatment of Zakah in 
the books of an Islamic financial institution (the institution), including the presentation and 
disclosure in its financial statements. 

The revision significantly alters the structure of the standard whereby the accounting and financial 
reporting principles provided are applicable depending upon the institutions obligation to pay Zakah. 

  Where an institution is not required by law or by virtue of its constitution documents to pay Zakah, it 

is still required to identify the amount of liability and disclose the same in respect of various 
stakeholders.

revision of AAOIFI FAS 9 “Zakah” was on the agenda and a broader scope revision was under 
consideration. This broader review scope included bringing consistency in various FASs and 
particularly with regard to the presentation and reporting of Zakah.  

A further review of AAOIFI FAS 9 “Zakah” and the preliminary study on the topic duly considering the

Control (of Assets and Business) 

Objective of the standard 

1. 

The objective of this standard is to establish the principles of assessing as to whether and when an 
institution controls an asset or a business, both in the case of underlying asset(s) of a participatory 
structure, as well as, for the purpose of consolidation of financial statements of subsidiaries. 

Scope 

2. 

3. 

This standard shall apply to all institutions preparing financial statements in line with the 
requirements of AAOIFI Financial Accounting Standards (FASs). 

This standard shall also be applicable to the entities being party to Shari’ah compliant transactions 
and structures (even if not included in the definition of an institution) insofar as allowed by the 
respective regulatory and reporting framework.  

Definitions 

4. 

For the purpose of interpreting and applying this standard, the following short definitions are 
relevant: 

a. 

b. 

c. 

Control – is as defined in paragraph 5; 

Participatory structure – is the type of contractual arrangement under which the parties to 
the contract share the profits, losses and / or residual interest in the net assets of the 
underlying assets or business; 

Quasi-equity – is an element of the financial statements that represents participatory 
contributions received by an institution on a profit sharing or participation basis. It has: 

i. 

ii. 

iii. 

primary characteristics of equity i.e., in case of loss (unless negligence / misconduct / 
breach of contractual terms is proved), the institution is not liable to return the lost 
funds to the fund providers and the fund providers share the residual interest in the 
underlying assets or business;  

certain characteristics of a liability i.e., it has a maturity or a put option of 
redemption / liquidation; and  

certain specific features i.e., the rights of the fund providers are limited only to the 
underlying assets or business and not on the whole of the institution, as well as, they 
do not have certain rights associated only with owners' equity.  

Control of assets and business 

5. 

An institution controls an asset or business, when it has substantially all risks and rewards incidental 
to ownership of such asset or business, duly meeting both of the following conditions: 

6 

a. 

b. 

it is directly exposed to, and has rights to, variable returns (negative and positive, 
respectively) from its involvement with such assets or business; and 

it has the ability to affect those returns through its power over the assets or business.  

Control of assets  
6. 

In line with the definition of control (see paragraph 5), an asset shall be considered to be in control 
of an institution when the institution has the present ability to direct the use of the asset and obtain 
economic benefits that may flow from the use of the asset. Control is also exhibited when the 
institution can prevent other parties from using the asset and obtaining economic benefits from the 
use of the asset. An institution usually obtains control over an asset once it acquires substantially all 
the risks and rewards incidental to ownership of the asset.   

Obtaining control of an asset 
7. 

An institution may obtain control of an asset (through having risks and rewards incidental to 
ownership of such asset) generally when: 

a. 

b. 

c. 

the asset is acquired as a consequence of consummation of a contract;  

the contract becomes legally binding on both parties; and 

the consideration (if any), is paid or becomes payable.

Preface 

PR1 

PR2 

In addressing the growing needs of the Islamic finance industry guidance for the first time 
adoption of AAOIFI FASs was deemed urgent. In response to inquiry from Islamic financial 
institutions in this respect, the AAOIFI Accounting and Auditing Standards Board (AASB) (as in 
existence at that time) issued a guidance note on the subject of first-time adoption of AAOIFI 
Financial Accounting Standards (FASs). It was decided to issue a guidance note rather than a full-
fledged standard with the same implementation effect as a full-fledged standard with the 
understanding that a decision to convert it to a comprehensive standard will be taken at a 
future date.  

The AAOIFI Accounting Board (the Board / AAB) in 2018 initiated the “AAOIFI Financial 
Accounting Standards (FAS) Review and Revision Project” in order to review all existing FASs and 
to revise them, if necessary. As a result, during the revision process of AAOIFI “Conceptual 
Framework for Financial Reporting by Islamic Financial Institutions” and the revision of FAS 1 
“General Presentation and Disclosure in the Financial Statements of Islamic Financial 
Institutions”, the Board also considered it relevant to initiate the revision of the Accounting 
Guidance Note No. 1 “Guidance Note on First Time Adoption of AAOIFI Accounting Standards by 
an Islamic Financial Institution”.  

PR3 

This was added to the Board agenda simultaneously due to its practical significance, especially in 
the jurisdictions that are in the phase of adopting, or are considering adopting AAOIFI FASs.  

4 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Introduction 

Overview 

IN1 

This financial accounting standard (FAS) ____ “First Time Adoption of AAOIFI Financial 
Accounting Standards” provides principles of financial reporting for Islamic Financial Institutions 
(the institutions), to be applied in the financial statements prepared for the first time according 
to the AAOIFI FASs, and to prescribe the transitional effects arising at the time of adoption. This 
FAS supersedes the AAOIFI Accounting Guidance Note No. 1 “Guidance Note on First Time 
Adoption of AAOIFI Accounting Standards by an Islamic Financial Institution” and should be read 
together with AAOIFI Shari’ah Standard No. 6 “Conversion of a Conventional Bank to an Islamic 
Bank”.  

Rationale for issuing this standard 

IN2 

IN3 

This FAS has been developed to address challenges that are faced by the institutions adopting 
AAOIFI FASs for the first time. It is expected that this FAS shall provide principle-based guidance 
to such institutions to smoothly transition from accounting frameworks other than AAOIFI FASs 
to AAOIFI FASs, and to prepare their first financial statements according to AAOIFI FASs. 

The Islamic finance Industry has grown exponentially in the past decade and newer markets are 
adopting AAOIFI FASs. Moreover, improvement opportunities were identified in the earlier 
guidance note, during the comprehensive review project initiated by AAOIFI for the existing 
FASs. Accordingly, the Board felt that an authoritative standard, instead of a guidance note, is 
now required to assist the institutions which are in the process to adopt the AAOIFI FASs or 
apply the same for the first time.

Session 2:  Islamic Finance and Governments: in the Changing Regional Economic En-

vironment

This session will feature two presentations on the topic which is quite important for the 
IFIs operating in most markets having strong Islamic finance presence, as well as their 
governments and fiscal policy makers. The floor will be open to the audience for discus-
sion and comments. Overall, the session aims to establish a link of the conference theme 
with the government fiscal policies and the budgetary deficits in the wake of economic 
changes brought in by the changing oil prices and the new vision of the regional gov-

ernments of planning for the post oil economic scenario. 

Main topics

•	 The existing and the possible role of Islamic finance in bridging the budgetary deficits.

•  Islamic finance and the government infrastructure projects – enhanced levels of inter-

action expected.

•  Role of sovereign Sukuk in region: from liquidity support to meeting the government 

funding needs.

•  Can Islamic finance meet the medium term financing needs of the regional govern-

ments – does it have the capacity and capability to handle that? 

•  New and innovative structures for supporting government funding needs.

Session 3: Agricultural Finance: the New Destination for Islamic Finance
This session will feature two presentations on the topic which is one of the key areas of
focus for Islamic finance in the times to come, particularly in the wake of the economic 
scenarios  impacted  by  the  oil  prices.  After  the  two  presentations,  two  distinguished 
panellists will provide their comments on the topic particularly touching upon the pres-
entations. The floor will later be open to the audience for discussion and comments. 

Main topics
•  What is the present status and statistics for Islamic finance in the agricultural sectors?
•  What is the Muslim countries GDP ratio coming from the agricultural and allied sec-

tors, and what are the gaps and opportunities for further growth?

•  How Islamic finance can play its role to fill that gap?
•  What products are now being used and what are the innovative products possible in 

future to fill that gap – at the institutional as well as the government level?

Session 4 :  Central  Shari’ah  Board,  its  Role,  Importance,  Risks  and  Evaluation  of         

Practical Experience 

While the Islamic banking and finance industry is moving towards it next stage of life,
the  regulators  across  the  globe  are  considering  the  need  of  Central  Shari’ah  Boards
for  improving  standardization  of  Islamic  finance  practices  and  having  an  eye  on  the
earlier  quite  liberal  and  diversified  approaches  of  Shari’ah  rulings  across  different 
institutions.  AAOIFI  has  also  brought  up  this  important  subject  on  its  agenda  of  the
Governance and Ethics Board. 

This session will feature two presentations / papers, commentator’s view, and discussion 
of the papers and broader session topic by two experts. The floor will be open to the 
audience for discussion and comments. 

Main topics
•	 What are the key considerations and requirements of the industry as well as that of 

the regulators?

Session 5:  Gold In Islamic Finance – AAOIFI Shari’ah Standard on gold in collaboration 

with	WGC	and	its	expected	impacts

With the growth of Islamic finance across the globe and given the increased confidence 

of  the  customers  as  well  as  common  issue  of  liquidity  management,  particularly,  the 

deposits heavy positions; Islamic finance is surely in need of innovative products and 

investments  avenues  in  particular.  Investment  in  gold  or  gold  products  is  one  of  the 

best suited options. AAOIFI and World Gold Council (WGC) have joined hands to develop 

a globally acceptable Shari’ah standard on the topic, which opens doors for numerous 

avenues for innovative products.

 The  session  will  include  presentations  from  WGC  and  the  prominent  Shari’ah  scholar 

involved in the process of development of the standard. Then there will be comments on 

the same by two distinguished experts and scholars before the floor gets open for public 

question and answer session.

Islamic Finance Products

The VAT treatment of Sharia-compliant financing products has an equivalent VAT 
treatment to the corresponding non-Sharia products to achieve the same economic result. 
This means that the implicit profit margin included in any fees or installments payable 
under a Sharia-compliant financing product may be treated as VAT exempt, even where 
this is part of an explicit fee or commission.

Any fee which relates to the provision of an underlying good or service to the recipient, or 
fees for administration of the arrangement, will be taxable.

finance company providing finance over that asset by way of purchase 
and onwards sale (lease to own) of that asset. The exact contractual

finance company acts in its own name in acquiring the asset – are 
essential to determine the associated VAT treatment.

Input Tax Recovery – Categories of Input Tax

Since the Banks provides a mixture of taxable and exempt supplies, it will be unable to fully recover all input tax it 
incurs in the course of business. 

Any non-recoverable input tax will form a business cost for the Banks. The Banks should consider undertaking a product 
repricing exercise in order to preserve its margins following the introduction of VAT. 
The recoverability of input tax on any particular purchase depends on which of the following categories it falls under:

1. Input tax directly and 
exclusively attributable to 
making taxable supplies
• Fully recoverable

2. Input tax directly and 
exclusively  attributable 
to making an exempt 
supply

• Fully non-recoverable

3. Input tax not directly 
and exclusively 
attributable to any 
particular type of supply
• Partially recoverable 
through the proportional 
deduction method (next 
slide)

It is anticipated that the majority of the Banks’s purchases will fall under Category 3.

Examples of purchases which fall under certain categories are as follows:

• The Bank receives an invoice with a VAT charge for the lease of branch.
Since the Bank provides both taxable and exempt supplies, this input tax falls under Category 3 and is therefore only 
partially recoverable through the proportional deduction method.

Proportional Deduction Method

Certain input tax incurred by a bank must be recovered through the proportional deduction method. Only the portion of 
input tax as determined by applying the recovery rate obtained through this formula is recoverable.

KSA:

Recovery Percentage =

Value of Taxable Supplies made by the Taxable Person over the last calendar year

Value of Taxable Supplies  + Exempt Supplies made over the last calendar year

UAE:  

Recovery Percentage =

Amount of Recoverable Tax (Category 1) incurred during this Tax Period

Amount of Recoverable Tax (Category 1) and Non-recoverable Tax (Category 2) 
incurred during this Tax Period
